(drum beat resonating) (atmospheric music)
(tone ringing) (audience clapping) - I'm Suze. Thank you so
much for joining us today. I'm Director of Researcher
and Community Engagement at Digital Science, and I have the utter privilege tonight for being joined by Venki Ramakrishnan. Venki is a Nobel Laureate. Venki is what we like to call
the scientific triple threat. You were a physicist by training. You're a structural biologist.
- Not for long. - Well, long enough, long
enough to get the badge. And you won the Nobel Prize in chemistry, so you're sort of the
Zac Efron of Science. - Which is really molecular biology. - Yeah. - So I'm neither a
chemist nor a physicist, but... (chuckles)
- I love it. It's me claiming not to be a physicist. I do that quite a lot. You're a former president of
The Royal Society as well, but now you're a group leader for the Structural Studies Division at the MRC's Laboratory
of Molecular Biology. This is a tiny fraction though, of what you've done and
what you've achieved. So I wonder, can I start with the
hardest question of tonight? Can you tell us a little
bit about your background and what brought you to writing this book? - Oh, well, (chuckles) okay. So I spent most of my life
studying the ribosome, which is the large molecular complex that translates our genetic
information into proteins. And it's all the thousands
of different types of proteins in each of our cells are made by translating
genetic information, and that's done by the ribosome. And that's really what
I was best known for. And it turns out though, that protein synthesis has
a lot of things about it. It's highly regulated. It controls the quality of proteins and all of these processes
break down when we age. So although I don't work on ageing myself, the process I study turns out to be of central importance to ageing. But the reason I wrote this book is that I was sort of ageing
adjacent in the sense I was in a field closely related to ageing, which had implications for ageing, but not part of the
ageing community myself. And it's a big existential question. You know, humans have
worried for centuries about, you know, why is it that
we grow old and die? Why do some species live for days or weeks and others live for hundreds of years? So this is a long-term
existential question. And for most of our existence as species, and by the way, I don't think animals
are aware of mortality. They're aware of death. They know when one of theirs has died. They even mourn their dead, some of them, but they don't know that there's an expiration date like we do. And so this is a big question. And for most of our existence, there's nothing we could do about it. You know, the saying goes, "Inevitable like death and taxes." But in the last 40 or 50 years, biology has made great
strides in understanding many of the underlying causes of ageing, what actually happens to
our molecules and our cells and organs as we age,
and what leads to death. And along with this, there's been a huge
explosion in ageing research. So I should say for quite a long time it was sort of a backwater in the molecular biology community. And you know, people, you know, mainstream molecular biologists
didn't really work on it. And it's only in the last 40 or 50 years that that's changed. And part of it is because we're all facing the issue of an ageing society where people are living longer. And so there's a great imperative
to make sure we're healthy as we age and we're
healthy in our old age. And so there's a lot
of money going into it. And at the same time, there's
a lot of private investment. There's 700 companies, startup companies doing some
aspect related to longevity. Some of them are selling
all sorts of supplements. They vary from very, very
serious based on rigorous science all the way to the other extreme. And then at the same time, there's an influx of
a lot of private money going into these companies, a lot of it from California
billionaires who I suspect, I mean, if you ask them, they say they're interested
in helping humanity and they want to improve
human health, et cetera. But I suspect that, you know, a lot of them are middle aged men worried about growing old, and that's generated a huge
amount of hype in the field. So you get, you know, extreme hype, people who think, you know, we're gonna live for hundreds of years and "The person who's going
to be a thousand years old has already been born." That's another statement. So I wanted to take an
objective look at the field, and because I don't
belong to the community, I don't have any financial interest, nor do I indeed have a
vested academic interest, none of my grants depend on,
you know, ageing research, so I can afford to be, you know, quite frank about calling
it the way I see it. So, you know, if I offend, you know, people in the ageing community, it's okay. They're not part of my community. So, you know. (chuckles)
(audience chuckling) So it's my effort to take
a frank look at the field and also, you know, humans are curious, or the hope is that it'll satisfy people's long standing
curiosity about this, you know, eternal phenomenon of ageing and death, this real existential question. And at the same time, it'll give them the tools to interpret new information
as it comes along. Next time you read a
new news item, it says, new cure for ageing, or, you
know, this, that, or the other, you can place that in the context of the fundamental biology
that I hope that the book will, you know, expose them to. - It is an incredibly emotive topic. And you can kind of understand
why I even struggled to say the D word just now when we were chatting just outside the lecture theatre, because it is quite anxiety-inducing and yet entirely inevitable. - Yeah, humans have always, you know, had this complex about death
and the finality of it. I think we've evolved to want to live partly because if you don't want to live, it's not very efficient. You won't pass on your genes unless you struggle and try to live. And even our language, we don't say somebody died, we say they passed on or they passed away, or they departed as if
it's some transition, you know, to some other station in life. And if you look at most religions, death is viewed as a transition. It's not viewed as an end. It's, you know, either you go to heaven, sometimes you corporally arise like in the Abrahamic religions, or your body may decay but
your soul is immortal and, you know, occupies some other body. But the idea is that you somehow live on after you, you know, given
up this particular body. So I think a lot of human culture is about denying the inevitability or the finality anyway of death. - Yeah. You talk about the
philosopher Stephen Cave and his four coping
strategies, but really, your book is this romp through the latest advancements
in molecular biology and the iceberg of research
on which they sit upon, to really address that
first one, which is, how do we extend that
life as long as possible? - Yeah. Stephen Cave had
a very interesting idea. He said we adopt four
strategies to deal with death and Plan A is simply not to die. And Plan B
(audience chuckling) is that you die, but
you can be corporally, you know, resurrected. And that's, you know, a lot of the Abrahamic religions used to traditionally insist on burial and they didn't like
cremation because of that. And the Plan C is, well, you can, you know, your body may decay, but you have an immortal soul
which can occupy other bodies or have an existence of its own. And Plan D is you live
on through your legacy, and the legacy could be your works or monuments you build, you know, you endow buildings after
yourself or build pyramids or it could be through
your offspring, you know. So, you know, biological
legacy as well as, you know, as other kinds of legacy. But you know, Woody Allen said he didn't want to live on in the hearts
and minds of, you know, people through his works, he wanted to live on in his apartment. So, you know,

is prevalent in this area where people really are desperate to try anything.
- Yeah. - You talk about the contradictions
in some of the research. You know, is red wine good for us? Maybe this week, maybe
not next week. Sorry, dad. Depends what week we're in. You talk about antioxidants, you know, how the role of the
mitochondria may be involved in sort of trying to reduce the number of free radicals that we have that could be potentially damaging. But then other studies
show that, you know, what doesn't kill you makes you stronger. Where are we going with this? Where is the sort of- - Yeah, I think, you know, this is true of any field of science that at the very frontiers
things are always a bit fuzzy and things you thought
were really happening turned out not to be true
and other things are true or things are true, but not
for the reasons you thought. So I wouldn't say the ageing
field is particularly different except because it gets
hyped so much, you know, people often have painted
themselves into a corner and then they don't want to give up their own ideas and so on. And maybe that's just human nature because Max Planck once
said that science, you know, new ideas and science don't emerge because people with the wrong
ideas change their minds in the light of new evidence. Rather, they just die, and eventually,
(audience chuckling) some new generation looks at the evidence, you know, with fresh eyes and moves on. And it's been paraphrased as "Science marches,
one funeral at a time." So, (chuckles)
(audience chuckling) and ageing research is
really, no different. You mentioned, you know, free
radicals and oxidative damage. I think there's no question
that oxidative damage and damage from, you know,
these reactive oxygen species. See our mitochondria
are where oxygen is used to oxidise sugars. So it's effectively where we
burn sugars to make energy. You can think of it that way, okay? Because you're producing CO2 and water just like you would if you took sugar and lit a flame to it and burned it, okay, except we're doing it
in a very controlled way at 37 degrees Celsius. And, you know, (object clattering) and using that energy in a
useful way, not just as heat, we're actually generating
useful energy out of it, useful chemical energy, but
that's where oxidation occurs. And if that oxidation's not
complete, we get these partial, you know, reduced oxygen molecules, which are called reactive oxygen species and they're highly reactive
and they can damage our DNA, they can damage other
parts of the mitochondria, they can leak out and damage
other parts of the cell. And so there's this big thing and people have showed, you know, that this is actually a cause of mitochondrial damage and of ageing. And so, you know, I think if you go to any
health food store today, you'll find loads of
antioxidant supplements, okay? And so you can see there's
some solid science behind it, but the fact is that
most of those supplements were shown not to work. And why is that? It's because maybe those supplements are not being used by our
body where they're needed and in the way that they're needed, okay? So it doesn't mean that reactive oxygen
species are not a problem. The other thing is, of course, when we can generate a small amount of reactive oxygen species,
our body senses that. They're also signalling molecules. They're not just damaging
molecules. We use it. Our body uses it for
signalling various chemical, you know, sending chemical signals. And so our body also
induces repair mechanisms and so they have the paradoxical effect of actually improving
things in smaller doses, but of course in larger
doses they could be, still, you know, still damaging. So I think it just shows you that you've got to take the
field in all its complexity and really understand what's going on and not just say, oh, these
things cause a problem, so we should go take antioxidants. You know, that's not a great solution. - Thank you, Ramakrishnan.
Thank you so much. (audience clapping)

RYVdDJ1cFrM.title: RYVdDJ1cFrM
RYVdDJ1cFrM.transcript:
[MUSIC PLAYING] [APPLAUSE] Well, hello. What a top crowd. How nice to see you all. My name is Vivian Perry and
you'll know who this is. Thank you, Ramakrishnan. And tonight we're going to be
talking about the Gene Machine, Venki's wonderful book. And if you haven't read it
yet, I urge you to read it. It's a great read. And this is going to be a
story of a career in science that could, at any moment,
have not ended as it has done. It's a story I think
that illustrates the march of science perfectly. It's the way that
intractable problems suddenly become solvable because
of advances in technology. It's a story of intense
competition and prizes. And finally, it's a story
about science and how it works, and this man,
Venki Ramakrishnan. And what I wanted to start with,
Venki, was a bit about you. Before we get to the
star of the show, which is of course the ribosome,
I wanted to take us back to where you started. So you were born
in Northwest India, your parents were scientists-- In South India. South India, sorry. Your parents were scientists,
but you didn't get into any top science schools. So if somebody had
come and seen you then, you would not be somebody
that they would have said, this is somebody who
is going to go far. Yeah, it's hard to say because
most bright students in India wanted to do
engineering or medicine. And they would go take
this big national exam. And there were all
these coaching classes that they would have to go to in
order to prepare for the exam. And my parents were
quite intellectual. They thought this
was all nonsense and, you know, they weren't
going to pay for any of that. And so I didn't do very
well on any of these exams and I didn't get into any
of these Indian institutes of technology, the IITs or one
of the top medical schools. But my mother wanted me to take
this other sort of exam, which was designed to identify
students who were good in science, basic science. And it was modelled after the
Science Talent Scholarship in the US, which for a long
time was called Westinghouse and then became Intel, and
now it's something else. I forget who's sponsoring it. But I took that, and I
got that scholarship. And one of the
conditions was that you had to do basic
science you couldn't do engineering or medicine. And so that's-- Which must of have been a bit
of a disappointment to your dad, because he-- Yes, he wanted me
to be a doctor. But I made a deal with him
that if I got the scholarship, I would do an undergraduate
degree in physics. And if I didn't get it, I
would go into medical school, our local medical school. And he said, fine. And somewhat to
his mixed feelings, I got the scholarship. So there you are, age 19. You disappear off to the US. But again, it's
not a great school. It's not a top school-- No. --that you go to. Yeah. So that was also a
weird thing, which was, you know, first of all,
I graduated from university when I was 19. And I wanted to go off to the
US because my parents were spending a summer there
on a short sabbatical. My dad was on a
short sabbatical. And I hadn't taken the GRE. So most of the top schools
wouldn't accept me. But this one place,
Ohio University said, you know, sure, we'll
give you a fellowship. And of course I
hadn't heard of it. And my father said, you know-- I think quite erroneously,
he said, well, you know, you can go anywhere. And if you work
hard, you'll be fine. You. Know and that was his attitude. And he still thinks-- you
know, he's 92-years-old and he thinks he was a prophet. [LAUGHING] But actually I THINK it's
completely the wrong thing. Because when you
go to the school-- a graduate school at any place,
it's all in your peer group that sort of drive you on
and motivate you and so on. So it's very important
to go to the best place you can get into. And it has to be said
that you didn't probably work quite as hard as you
could have done at that stage. NO. I think part of the
problem is the first two years in physics graduate
school are coursework and then you take these exams. And I took these exams and
that's when I realised I wasn't really cut out to be
a physicist, because they, as you know, they asked me,
so what are you interested in? And I could really tell them. And then they said,
well, have you read anything interesting
at all, you know, in the last few months? And I just sort of really
think hard and then come up with this lame
topic that I'd seen on the cover of a magazine. And so I realised, you know,
I realised actually maybe I wasn't so interested in physics. And I often say-- so I have all these
other interests. I became a chess player and
hopped freight trains, which is illegal, but there you are. See, all these things
you're learning about this splendid gentlemen
who hopped freight trains. Just remember that. [LAUGHING] Anyway. Essential prerequisite
to the Nobel Prize is hop a freight train. So I often say, you know, if I
had graduate students like me, I would just fire them. [LAUGHING] And you also got married. I mean-- Yes. --you were a very young man. And you married and you and
Vera had a child already then. So there you were-- 23-years-old, I was married
with a six-year-old stepdaughter and, you know, a baby. It was about six weeks old. And I was off to start
my second career. And why did you choose biology? So when I was in physics
and I was disillusioned with a mixture of my
problem and possibly my own aptitude for it, I used
to read Scientific American. And I would see all these
fascinating articles on biology. And it seemed as though
biology was at the stage that physics had been in the
early 20th century with quantum mechanics and relativity and
atomic structure and so on. So I thought, you know, this
would be really fascinating. And then I also knew
that lots of physicists like Francis Crick
or Max Delbruck or Max Perutz, who founded
the LMB where I work. All of these people had made
that sort of transition. And so I decided maybe
this would be worth doing. And luckily Vera, you know,
was game to sort of humour me in going back to graduate school
and living on a very modest graduate student stipend
with two children in Southern California without a car. So I think having a
supportive spouse was really quite important for it. And we're going to
hear quite a lot more about various support for you
over the years and how crucial that was. So there you were. You'd come out of physics. You'd only done two
years in biology, and you decide that you're
going to work on ribosomes. And tell us then what was
known about the ribosome at that particular time. Yeah. So when I went to graduate
school in biology again-- first of all, most
places wouldn't accept me because I already had a PhD. But a few places did. And the University of California
at San Diego I thought had the best
combination of quality and a place where I could
have two young children. And so I went there and I
started learning about DNA and genes and realised
that actually DNA coded for proteins. All of these things
that we call genes are actually bits
of information that allow you to make proteins. But the way that it's
made is that if you think of DNA as this
archive of our genes, just as if you go to
the British Library, they're not going to take
out their valuable books. They're going to make you make a
photocopy or a microfilm of it, and then you work with the copy. And so the cell
does the same thing. It has this archive tucked
away in the nucleus, which contains all its genes. And then as it needs particular
genes, it makes a copy of it called messenger RNA. And that's exported
from the nucleus out into the cytoplasm,
the rest of the cell, and then this very
large molecule. And large and
small are relative, so it's tiny by human standards. You know, if you take the
width of a human hair, you could line up 20,000
of these molecules along the width of a human hair. So it's very small
by our standards. But by molecular
standards it's enormous. It has a million atoms. And so, you know, it's
called a ribosome. And it's this very
ancient machine. And what it does is it reads
the messenger RNA, which is essentially like a sentence
in a four-letter alphabet, which is the four types of
bases that DNA and RNA have. And using that,
reading that sentence and reading three
letters at a time, it stitches together
a different kind of chain, which is
the protein chain. So the protein chain is this
long chain of amino acids, and there are 20 types of them. And each gene has a different
order of these 20 amino acids. So you can think of hundreds
and hundreds, thousand of individual beads
with different beads in different orders. And the miracle is-- you take this long chain-- and
in this very theatre in 1968, David Phillips showed the
structure of a lysozyme, which was the third protein to
be solved, which is right here. Now-- We just happened to have
one we made earlier. --when he showed what this
chain would look like, if it was extended, it came down
from the ceiling to the floor. OK. And how does it know how
to fold up into this shape? Well, it's the order of the
amino acids in the chain that miraculously contains the
information to allow the chain to fold up into its shape. And it's that folded shape
that gives each protein its special function. Because it's the
shape of the protein, the structure of it
that allows it to work. And so we have all
sorts of proteins. You have a protein in your
eye that allows you to see, called rhodopsin. You have another protein
called haemoglobin, which allows you to carry
oxygen from your lungs in through your blood. You have a long
filamentous protein called collagen, which makes
up your skin and connective tissues. Greg Winter got the Nobel
Prize this year for work on antibodies. Well antibodies
are also proteins, and they help fight
off infections. So we can hear because
of proteins in our ears. We can sense touch because
of proteins in our nerves and so on. So proteins carry out
thousands of functions. Every one of them is made by the
ribosome reading instructions in the gene for that protein. So you've got this factory, the
ribosome, and information comes in, and product goes out. Exactly. And something goes on in it. And in the 1970s that
was pretty much it. That was what you
knew at the time. Yes. I would say a bit was known
in the sense, the players that came into the ribosome and
brought in the amino acids were these small adaptor molecules. That was known. It was known that it read them
in blocks of three at a time, and it was known that
other proteins would have to come into the
ribosome at different points and help it do something
and go on to the next step. But the problem was, they
couldn't see any of this. They didn't know
anything about what these molecules looked like. And so it was a
bit of a black box. And I say in the book, you know,
imagine if you're a Martian and you hovered over
the Earth and you saw these little objects move
around in straight lines. And then if you
look closely you'd see there were even
smaller objects that would enter these objects. And the object
would only move when there are these small objects
inside the bigger object. And when the little objects
got out, the object would stop. And you wouldn't know
anything about it. And then you'd get
closer and you'd say, oh, it emits carbon dioxide and
water, and it uses up gasoline. So you'd get a little bit
of an idea of this thing. But unless you actually
looked at the thing and saw it had an engine and a
steering wheel and, you know, crankshaft and so on, you would
have no idea how it worked. And it was the same
with the ribosome. It was just this
sort of black box that seemed to
miraculously do this thing. And you knew that structure
was essential to function. Yeah. So it's, unless you
knew its structure, you wouldn't be able to
work out its function. Yeah. And that's been a
theme-- you know, the idea that you have to see
something to understand it, that's been a theme
for hundreds of years. So, you know, when Galileo
could see moons of Jupiter and so on and planets,
that gave a huge impetus to understanding
the solar system, the Heliocentric theory. And later on when Robert
Hooke was able to see-- or van Leeuwenhoek were
able to actually see microbes and cells,
we got the idea that all life consists of
units formed from cells. So in every case, seeing
the next level of detail has transformed the field. And in a classic example
of molecular structures, the double-helix of DNA,
before that we had no idea how heredity could be transmitted
and how information could-- molecules could even
carry information. So when you saw the double-helix
structure, suddenly you had, for the first time
in centuries-- I mean, people have been
wondering for centuries, how come your children
are like their parents and how come we don't give birth
to sheep or things like that? And so, you know, I
think for the first time those sorts of
things became clear. And it was the same
with other molecules. So let's park the
ribosome for a moment. It's this black box,
and go back to Venki. And the thing that
really changed your life was reading an article in
the Scientific American about neutron scattering. Yeah. I'm afraid not at all
a useful technique in biology, although
some people who use it and do it for a living
will probably be outraged. But anyway. But it was on the ribosome. And there were two things
that drew me to the article. One was, one of the
authors was Don Engelman. He was one of two
people at Yale who had offered me a post-doctoral
fellowship straight out of my PhD in physics. And I turned him down saying,
I don't know any biology, and I need to learn
some biology before I could do that sort of thing. And the other was, I knew
the ribosome was important. And then the third thing
was that I thought, well, neutron scattering-- I have a background in physics,
so I can probably pick that up. And so maybe I'll
be useful to them. And so I wrote to Don
and I said, you know, you offered me a
job two years ago when I didn't know anything. And now I actually
know some biology. So maybe you'll
want me even more. So he put me in touch with his
collaborator, Peter Moore, who was really the ribosome expert. And that's how I
ended up going to Yale to work on the ribosome. So at this time, really
nobody was interested much in ribosomes. It was a kind of niche interest. And let's be fair, that
technique didn't really work. Yeah. And it was suggested, in
the nicest possible way, that it was time to move on. Yes. And so I must just quote
this from your book, because I just think
it's a wonderful phrase. So, "The universities
looked at my career-- a bachelor's and doctorate
in physics, neither of which were from a prestigious
university, two years studying biology without a
degree, followed by research using a technique
no one had heard of, to work on an old problem that
was already unfashionable." It wasn't looking
good, Venki, was it? No. So when I applied-- so until then, it was a
sort of natural progression, except for this little
deviation into biology. You do a PhD, you do a postdoc. That's all pretty natural,
except that I'd done a detour and switched
fields, by switching from physics to biology. But anyway, after
a postdoc is when scientists face the big crunch. It's at that point
that they either have to get a
faculty job or they have to get a
different type of job or they have to
do something else. And I applied for 50 positions. And I applied to everything
from four-year colleges. So America has very good
four-year colleges-- these are undergraduate
teaching institutions, which don't do a
lot of research-- as well as, sort of
mediocre universities and a few top universities. And out of those
50 applications I got exactly zero
interviews because of this. I bet it'd be like the man
who turned down the Beatles, isn't it? Anyway. But I say in the book that
the four-year colleges were interested mostly in teaching. They probably looked at
my long Indian name and-- they knew I had grown up in
India-- they probably thought, we don't even know
if this guy can speak English, let alone teach. So they didn't want me. And then the top
research universities probably-- you know, they
looked at my rather chequered CV and they said,
well, you know, this is some outlier and
not worth considering. I don't blame them actually. I mean, probably if I'd had
been on the selection committee, I'd of done the same thing. And then you went to Oak Ridge,
which was a bit disastrous, wasn't it? I did. There was 15 months. I went there-- my
advisor, Don Engelman had called up this
fellow who was a physicist, who was
running a neutron scattering facility in Oak Ridge. And they wanted a biologist to
sort of help attract people. So in effect I was a neutron
salesman, if you like. You know, attract biologists to
come and use this instrument. So I said, I'm
happy to do that as long as I can do my own work. And they said, yes. We'll set you all up with a lab. And then the lab
never materialised. So out of 15 months-- well, three months after I got
there, I was looking for a job again. So the first decade of
your career as a scientist was not looking promising. No. It was terrible, because I
had started off in physics and then I'd sort of, you know-- there's an American
term called, "punting," which is different from the
Oxford and Cambridge punting. I means, you know,
when you know you can't score with your football,
you use your free kick and give up the ball
to the other team. But it means sort of
starting over, basically. So I had to start
over in biology. So it was effectively
my second career. Now I found my second career
wasn't working out either, you know. So it was, you know,
how many lives did I have was the question. So let's, again, park that. So Venki at this point is,
it's not looking good, ladies and gentlemen. It really isn't. Circling the plug
hole, is, I think what the Americans call it. So let's turn to crystals and
the basic techniques which were around at the time for
looking at both-- well, DNA people are very familiar with
the story of Rosalind Franklin and the crystallography. Just tell us why you
needed to have crystals in order to work out the
structure of a molecule. So normally, you
know, if you want to look at something
very small you would look at it with
a magnifying glass. Or if you wanted to look
at it in even more detail, you would use a
microscope, which is really a series of
magnifying glass lenses. And the way that
works is that light scattered from the object. And what the lens
does is it collects the rays that are scattered and
combines them into an image. And in fact all of you
are doing this right now while you're looking at
us, because there are lenses in your eyes which are taking
the scattered rays from me and then recombining them
to an image in your eye. And that's how you see. And with a lens you can
make the image much bigger than the object itself. So then you can look
at the magnified image and see details. But the problem is that if
you want to look at molecules, the distance between
atoms in a molecule is far too small to use light. Because there's an
old theorem in physics that you can't
distinguish objects that are further apart than
half the wavelength of the light you're using. And light has many
thousands of times longer wavelength than the
distance between atoms. So you would need a much
shorter wavelength radiation. So if you take light and you use
light of the kind of wavelength that corresponds to the
distance between atoms, well that is what
we call x-rays. So x-rays and ordinary light
are both the same thing. They're just different
energy and wavelength. But the trouble is,
there's no lens for x-rays. And even if there were, x-rays
are unlike ordinary light. It's actually damaging. So by the time you
were able to use x-rays to magnify
one single molecule, you would fry the molecule. You would destroy it before
you could even see it. So the way around that was
invented by Lawrence Bragg while he was a PhD student. And he was--
actually I believe he was the director of the Royal
Institution in the '60s. Anyway, he figured out that
if you hit a beam of x-rays to a crystal, which
is essentially a three-dimensional
stack of molecules, then you could take
the scattered rays and you could computationally
do what a lens does. So if in effect you
do calculations, you'd measure the intensities
of the scattered rays and do what a lens does, but
you would do it in a computer instead. And you could then,
in a computer, calculate a
three-dimensional image. And that's how
crystallography began. And it started off with
a very small molecule, sodium chloride-- common salt,
which has only two atoms. That's what Lawrence
Bragg started with. And then it went
to bigger molecules and Dorothy Hodgkin was
a big exponent of it. And she worked on molecules
which had a few hundred atoms, like vitamin B12. At the time it was
a tour de force and got her a Nobel Prize. And also a headline
in the Daily Mail which said, "Oxford
Housewife Wins Nobel." [LAUGHING] An Oxford housewife
and mother of three. Mother of three. Yes. [LAUGHING] Yeah. I quote that in the book. But then if you get
to proteins, proteins have a few thousand atoms
instead of a few hundred atoms. The techniques of Dorothy
Hodgkin wouldn't be useful. And Max Perutz was in Cambridge
was working on this problem. he worked on it for
23 years before he made his breakthrough. And he and his former
student, John Kendrew, but then his collaborator,
worked on the first two protein structures, which were
haemoglobin and myoglobin. And they won the
Nobel Prize in 1962. And they'd figured
out techniques to solve these much
larger molecules. And the third protein
structure was done here at the Royal Institution
by David Phillips, and that's lysozyme. That was about six years
after Kendrew and Perutz won the Nobel Prize
for the first two. But you're making
it sound a bit easy. And it's-- It wasn't easy,
because, you know-- Because the first-- Yeah. So the first protein structures
took about 23 years, you know, because you had to figure
out all the methods, and even how to collect data. And there were also a bit
lucky in that digital computers were invented right around
the time when they needed it. And Kendrew, who doesn't
get a lot of credit-- I mean, he's one of the more
under-recognised people. He also started
EMBL, by the way, which is one of the
big European labs. Anyway, he realised that
you needed to use digital computers. And in Cambridge there
was this huge computer called EDSAC, you know, which
would take up almost as much as this room, practically. And they would have to go there
to do all their calculations. Of course now I
have many millions more orders of magnitude more
computing power in my pocket, as do most of you. But that helped. And this goes back
to this technology, you know, driving science. And then it took another six
years to do the third protein structure. And for a long time protein
structures would take, you know, typically five to
10 years for each one to do. But then the pace accelerated. And the other thing that you
had to do, which is, crystals are pesky things. That's right. I mean, really pesky things. Yeah. I mean, you might
think it's easy if you left a bit of salt
out and it's salty water and it sort of crystallises. But actually getting crystals-- It's a huge step. It's really difficult. It's a difficult step because
what you're trying to do is you're coaxing molecules
to form these very regular three-dimensional arrays. Now if you have spheres,
you know, regular spheres like marbles of the same size,
you can easily stack them up. OK. Well if you have some,
say, floppy teddy bears or something, it's
much, much harder to make a very regular stack. They'll all be
slightly different. They won't lined
up very precisely. And so you can think of
proteins as sort of floppy-- you know, this looks rigid,
but actually in water it's in solution, and it's actually
kind of slightly floppy. And to get this sort of-- this has a few thousand
atoms-- to get it to line up in crystals is very,
very difficult. And the idea that a ribosome,
which is a million atoms-- so imagine, this is a
few thousand atoms. That's a million atoms. So it's actually many times
bigger than this lysozyme-- to get that to
crystallise was not thought to be really
straightforward at all. But Ada Yonath, who was a
scientist who was, at the time, I think in Berlin, had
managed to get a crystal. That's right. So Ada Yonath went to work
with Heinz-Gunter Wittmann, who ran a big department, almost
like an institute in Berlin. He was a Max Planck director. And he was interested
in all things ribosomal. And he actually had tried
to get a couple of people to give this problem a go,
which is to try and crystallise a whole ribosome. And one of them
turned out to be a bit of a charlatan, and the other-- and he left. And the other guy wanted
to come to work on it, but his girlfriend was
German so he wanted to spend time in Germany. And his girlfriend at
some point dumped him. So he didn't want to go anymore. So Wittmann had this fellowship. So when Ada Yonath
wrote to him, he already had a fellowship lined up. So he simply
transferred it to her. She came to Berlin,
and the first year she was working on something else. And then as she narrated,
she had a bicycle accident or something and she was
hospitalised for a while. And while she was
there she was thinking, well, maybe I should
give whole ribosomes a shot, because, you
know, the Institute is making lots and
lots of these ribosomes from different species. So when she told Wittmann,
he was naturally delighted because it had been on his mind. And he didn't
think anybody would be willing to sort of
take on the project, because it seemed so impossible. But she, to her credit, not only
took it on, but stuck with it for quite a long time. So the ribosome is-- it's composed of a big bit, a
little bit, and some proteins. Yeah. So if you look up
there, you see there's a bottom part in
yellow, and a top part, in sort of light blue. And so the top part is what's
called the large subunit, and the bottom part
is the small subunit. They don't have much
originality, scientists-- the big bit, the little bit. Anyway, so the big bits and
the little bit, and they're going to feature largely
in our story to come. So we've got-- Ada Yonath has
got some crystals. You now decide that you're
going to go to the Laboratory Molecular Biology in
Cambridge and you're going to do a sabbatical for a year. So poor Vera gets
dragged across-- Oh, no, no. I wouldn't say
poor Vera for that. You know, Vera is an extreme
anglophile, you know. I call her the English
woman from Ohio. so I don't think there was
any sort of imposition at all. In fact, she had spent a summer
in Durham when she was 17 and had been in love

thing about the ribosome. It's so fundamental. It's at the crossroads
between genes and the proteins that are
what the genes code for. And I like to say that
every molecule in every cell was either made by

ancient RNA world. It sort of emerged

- Good evening, and welcome to those here joining with us in person
in Elebash Recital Hall and to those joining us online. I'm Josh Brumberg, president
of the CUNY Graduate Center. Welcome everyone to us
tonight for a discussion with Nobel Laureate,
Dr. Venki Ramakrishnan. This event is part of an annual
series held in partnership with Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory Center for Humanities and
History of Modern Biology and the BGI Laureate Archives Program. Tonight we'll hear from a
world-renowned scientist about his journey of discovery. He won the Nobel Prize in 2009
for his breakthrough work, mapping the structure of ribosomes, the protein factories of cells. More recently, he has devoted his molecular biology expertise to exploring and explaining
the science of aging, which he writes about in
his illuminating new book, "Why We Die: The New Science of Aging "and the Quest for Immortality." Thanks to science, many of us can live
longer, healthier lives than ever before in human history. Understanding how we age is crucial for our own
health and wellbeing, and for those we care about
and for the society we live in. I am grateful to Dr. Ramakrishnan for sharing his findings

bare minimum of calories and any nutrients they need, like vitamins and protein and so on. And these animals tend to
resemble younger animals when they're old and they also extend
lifespan by about 30% or so. So I mean, 30% would mean, if we're 85, 30% of that is significant. It's about 20 more years, right? So there are people who
advocate caloric restriction, and there are a few
people who practice it, but I can tell you it's extremely hard. The animals who are on
caloric restriction, the moment you take them off that diet, they start gorging as
much as they can eat. They're hungry all the time. They feel cold, they
have a loss of libido. So, it reminds me of the doctor who says, "Do these things and
you'll live a lot longer." And the patient says, "Really? "Doc, if I just do these things, "will I actually live 10 years longer?" And the doc says, "Well, I'm not sure, "but it'll feel like it." (audience laughing) So anyway. So that's caloric restriction. But the alternative to that is to ask what does caloric restriction do? And we have a pretty good idea of some of the major things
that happen in our cells as a result of caloric restriction. And then you ask, can you find drugs that have the same effect
as caloric restriction so that you can pop a pill and then still have your
blueberry pie and ice cream? And one of those pills is rapamycin. Rapamycin inhibits a major
pathway called the TOR pathway, which is related to my old
field because it shuts down, prevents ribosomes from
making protein on mRNA. But that's a different story. But anyway, rapamycin was
discovered completely by accident as an antifungal compound. Then it turned out to be thought of as maybe an anti-cancer compound. And then people found out that actually it was an immunosuppressor, so it was approved as a drug to give organ transplant recipients to prevent rejection of a
foreign organ inside them. Now, the idea of giving
an immunosuppressive drug for prolonged periods to
somebody so they won't age, seems slightly problematic to me because they'll be more
prone to infections. They have all various other side effects, like if they get injured, they're more slow to heal the wound because that requires protein synthesis. So, I think it's problematic. But the advocates of
rapamycin are very gung-ho because of animal trials. And they say, "Well, we
can adjust the dose down "so that we don't have
these bad side effects, "but we'll still get the
benefit of rapamycin." And there are some studies now indicating that that might be possible. But I still think it's one of those cases where you need very long-term studies. Now, what was the other
thing you mentioned? Oh, metformin. Yeah, so metformin is a
somewhat different category because it's given to
millions of people already for Type 2 diabetes. So we know that it's a
relatively safe drug, but it does have side effects. It is at some level, everything is... Whether something is a toxin
or not depends on the dose. Okay? I mean, even water is a toxin
if you drink too much of it. So, the point is that can you use metformin
to extend healthy life? This came from an early study
that diabetics on metformin were living longer than non-diabetics, which was actually counterintuitive because diabetes is a
risk factor in aging. And so that led to a lot
of interest in metformin. But other studies didn't quite support, in fact came up with almost
the opposite conclusion. So the studies on metformin
and their effect on health span is somewhat mixed. And proper control trials need to be done. And in fact, Nir Barzilai,
who's at Einstein, has actually, is trying to initiate a
human trial on healthy humans to take metformin, and
monitoring them over a period. And this brings to mind a general problem with aging therapeutics. If you have cancer,
you'd be willing to take really nasty, horrible drugs
with horrible side effects, highly toxic drugs, because
the alternative is you'll die. But an anti-aging
therapeutic is different. You're asking somebody to
take it for 10 or 20 years in the hope that at the very end they'll get another 10
years of healthy life. And that's a different proposition. So I think the bar for anti-aging
therapeutics is different from serious life-threatening disease. - So, in that same vein, there's currently an XPRIZE competition to revolutionize how we
approach human lifespan. They're offering $101 million for a proactive, accessible therapeutic that reduces the risk of
chronic age-related diseases, increases human lifespan, and extends the quality of
life well into our later years. Now, since you will be living to 120, is there a particular moonshot
approach in aging research that you see or you might be interested in
pursuing in the next decades? - Well, no, I'm not gonna
pursue anything, but the... Well, there are many approaches. You talked about caloric restriction, that's resulted in a whole
class of drugs, like... I would consider metformin
roughly in that category, even though it's not directly related to caloric restriction. Another one of these, GLP-1 drugs, which were originally
for morbidly obese people or people with really severe
weight-related diabetes, but now they're finding that they have generally positive effects on all kinds of things, including aging of the
central nervous system. And so that's a possibility. But they also come with downsides. They lead to loss of muscle mass. And that leads to frailty and that's a big problem as you age. So again, these are problems. I would say the one area that
I think is very exciting, but also very challenging, is this idea of reprogramming cells. And what I mean by that is
when you have a fertilized egg, there's a program that's set in place that results in the fertilized egg differentiating into an
embryo, growing into an embryo. Each of those cells in the early embryo has the ability to form any
of the tissues in the body. But as the embryo develops
it differentiates further into specialized so-called stem cells, which can then only give rise
to certain classes of cells. Like some cells can give rise to all of the cells of the blood system, including our immune
system and red blood cells. Others can form all the
cells in our nervous system. So, it becomes more and more specialized. Now, when you get to the final stage where you have fully
differentiated tissue, you have skin and cartilage and bones and hair and organs and blood and so on, they can't go backwards. You can't go from there
back to a fertilized egg. So, it's essentially a
unidirectional process. But it turned out you can
actually go backwards. John Gurdon, who unfortunately
died at 92 just last week, showed when he was very young, I think he was just a graduate student, he showed that you could
take an adult cell, remove the nucleus
containing all its genes, and put it in an egg from which the nucleus had been removed, and that would grow into a
clone of the original animal from where you took the skin cell. That was the first cloned experiment, and eventually they did it in mammals, and you got Dolly the sheep. That shows that you can
take that final skin cell and have it go all the way back and develop into a whole new animal that's essentially a genetic
copy of the original animal. It's a clone. And then 50 years later, a Japanese scientist named Yamanaka showed that if you introduced
genes for four factors, these are factors that affect other genes, they can turn on or off other genes called transcription factors. So if you take four
genes, particular genes, and introduce them into a cell, you somehow start rewiring the cell so it starts going backwards. And of course, if you did this
to every cell in the body, we'd all end up with a horrible mess. Everything would be like a starting cell. You'd lose all the
identity of the tissues. And so you wouldn't wanna do that. But the question is, can you turn on these factors
for just a short while so that our tissue sort of
goes reverse in development for just a little bit,
and just short enough that they're still
maintaining their identity? So a particular skin
cell stays a skin cell, or a hair follicle cell
stays a hair follicle cell. And I would've thought this
was an outlandish idea, but people have tried this in mice and they've shown that
these mice looked healthier by many criteria, including
those clocks we talked about. And so there's a whole community now trying to use this kind of reprogramming to see if they can use that to maybe reverse some
of the effects of aging. I think it's a long way
from these animal trials simply because of the
way it was switched on and the cancer risks
associated with it and so on. It's still a long wait before
you can use it in humans. But given the amount of money
and the quality of scientists that have gone into this field, I wouldn't be surprised if they made some sort of breakthrough in the next decade or so. - Now, there's ample data that suggests that human lifespan is
affected by social class. So, as we think about these interventions that are gonna increase our
health span and lifespan and perhaps our maximum lifespan, how do we think about this inequality in social class and lifespan? And how do we ensure that
the benefits accrue to all? - So, I wanna talk about two things. One is the disparity. And the second is what
would it do to society if we all started living
to be a 100 or 120? And that's being modest by... I mean, some of these people claim that the first person to live to be 150 has already been born. And one person at the extreme end, a British scientist who
now lives in California with a very long beard, says that the first person to live to be a 1,000 has already been born, okay. So, disparity first. If you look at the top 10%
of population in Britain, economically, the richest
10% versus the poorest 10%, the life expectancy
difference is 10 years. So the richest 10% are
living 10 years on average more than the poorest 10%. That's in a country with
a national health service. In America, it's 15 years, okay. And if you look at the fraction of years that they spend in good health, that number's almost double. So that means the poor are
not only living shorter lives, but they're spending more
of it in poor health. So that's a big disparity. I should point out. My stepdaughter, who
works in public health, pointed out that in New York City, the difference in life expectancy between the richest and
poorest zip codes is 35 years. Okay? And not all of this is due to aging. A lot of it is due to crime, poor medical care, poor nutrition, there's a whole series of external causes, but a good part of it
is also due to aging. And so that's the reality today. Now, if you have
sophisticated interventions like stem cells that I
was telling you about, this is not gonna be as easy necessarily as popping a pill like a statin. I mean, maybe it'll come
to that, but not at first. And so who's gonna avail themselves of these therapeutics first? It's gonna be the people
who can afford it. And so you're going to increase
that disparity even more, and that's going to
create real social issues. Now, there's a social issue anyway, because as you get older, you
accumulate a number of things. You accumulate wealth, you accumulate power and influence, all of which are interconnected. And so if you have a society... We already have a society
where people are living longer, but fertility rates are dropping, so you're not having as many
young people in the population. So you're having a society where
turnover is extremely slow, which means the same people who have power are just sort of hanging on. And we see this in, I'm sure in your
university as well as mine, you see, especially in academia, you see these people
hanging on running big labs and not showing no signs of
stopping, okay. (chuckles) And that's just an issue, you know. So there's a question of
intergenerational fairness. The other problem is I think creativity is something that is
optimal when we're young. And it's not simply because
we're biologically young, it's because when we're young, we're facing the world
fresh for the first time. We're looking at things new. We've not accumulated a lifetime of prejudices and dogma and so on, and so we're more open-minded. And that's part of being creative is being receptive to new ideas. And this is why when you look at science, people continue to do
good work later in life. But if you ask when they die, what are they going to be remembered for? And if you ask that, you'll find that most of
their big breakthroughs was much earlier in their lives. And it's not just science and math. Kazuo Ishiguro said in a public meeting, a public interview, but also told me that
it's true in literature. If you look at authors and ask, what was their big famous work, like "War and Peace" by Tolstoy, it was done when they
were typically under 40. So, I think, this life extension is going to create all
sorts of social issues that we haven't thought about. - Okay, let's see. Many folks who heard that
I was hosting you today asked me to make sure that I
asked you about some things, and to separate fact from fiction. There's probably too many
for us to go through, but let me just mention a few of them and then you can comment. So the Mediterranean
diet, the yogurt diet. I also learned that there are
blue zones all over the world where there's a high
concentration of centenarians, Loma Linda, California being
the one in the United States. And let's start there. - I'll talk about the blue zones first because I'm really glad that I didn't write about them in my book. Because I might have been tempted to somehow explain blue zones. But the reality is, a paper
was published about a year, I think a year and a half
ago, which analyzed... Remember, you're talking about people who live to be over a 100. So you're looking at record
keeping in all of these places from a hundred years ago. Okay? And when you look at how
accurate the records were and throw out all the ones where there was uncertainty in the age, the effect goes away. (audience laughing) And so this person actually
got the Ig Nobel Award, which Harvard gives out for interesting kind of quirky science. But, I will tell you one thing that made me
skeptical about blue zones is they were so different. You had people in Sardinia in Italy, or Loma Linda in California,
or Okinawa in Japan, and they all were different genetically. They were different in
their lifestyle and diet and all sorts of things. So, I'm very skeptical about that. On the other hand, we know from
caloric restriction studies that a healthy diet is important. So it could be that the Mediterranean diet is a balanced diet, which has got a rich mixture
of fresh fruits and vegetables and fiber and things like that. And presumably they don't overeat. And so maybe it's that combination. Italy, by the way, is at
the vanguard of aging. So in terms of an aging population, it's ahead of the rest of
Europe, ahead of the U.S., but it's sort of like South Korea in terms of people living very long and not having very many children. - And then there's the red wine
and resveratrol connection. - Yeah, so I mean. I have to be very careful what I say here because what I wrote in the book was carefully vetted by two lawyers. So...
(audience laughing) But I should say, this came out of
discoveries made in Boston. But later studies have really cast doubt on whether resveratrol
actually has an effect on these proteins called sirtuins, which are thought to promote longevity. And then the question of whether sirtuins actually
promote longevity has also been questioned. And so this thing has turned
out to be a house of cards that most aging researchers
are quite skeptical of it. And by the way, the amount of red wine
you would have to consume to equal the amount of
resveratrol in these studies would be like hundreds of bottles. And so it's not that the
French are living longer because of their red wine. - Okay, let's turn to a few
questions from the audience. So, there's a question that asks if you could please say
more about your journey from physics to biology to chemistry. - Well, the last part isn't true actually. I like to joke that ever since they gave the chemistry
Nobel Prize to Rutherford, which was in 1908, it's been known that you don't
need to know any chemistry to get the Nobel Prize in chemistry. (audience laughing) No, but I did go from being a physicist to a molecular biologist. I think part of the reason was that I was working on a problem
that I was not interested in. I didn't feel I had any aptitude for it. And I was reading
popular science articles, mainly in Scientific American,
this was in the seventies. And I realized that biology was still
undergoing a huge revolution, and it's still going on, by the way. And at that time, DNA was being sequenced
for the first time. We we're getting our glimpse at what biological molecules look like, what membranes look like,
what the immune system, the basis of how the immune system worked, or nerve transmission
and things like that. So, everything seemed,
like, incredibly exciting. So I decided to sort of punt
and just bail out of physics and start graduate school again. - Okay. Let's see. So in cancer, one of
the hallmarks of cancer is that the cells begin to divide and they live without inhibition. And the question here is, can we harness the underlying
process in a good way? - Yeah, people, when
telomerase was discovered there were sort of two camps. One was that aha, if we
could inhibit telomerase, then these cancer cells wouldn't be able to divide indefinitely, and we'd have a sort of
universal target for all cancers. And although telomerase was
discovered some decades ago, that's not been one of the
more promising avenues. I mean, some of the
biggest therapies in cancer have not come out of that. The other attitude was if telomere loss is a problem with aging, maybe we can reactivate
telomerase in these cells and extend these telomeres
and thereby help with aging. And there are a number of scientific labs and also many companies that are formed with precisely that idea. But if you talk to world
famous telomere experts like Titia de Lange just uptown from here, she will tell you that telomere biology is an intricate balance
between aging and cancer, and tinkering with it could dramatically also
increase the risk of cancer. And by the way, the
person who lived to be 117 was a record holder who just
died a couple of years ago, from Spain, from I think Barcelona. She wanted everything about her studied. And so the scientists, biomedical scientists actually
analyzed all of her tissues and her DNA and her methylation patterns and all sorts of things, and published an article in Cell just two or three weeks ago. And she had shorter telomeres than usual. So, that doesn't go well with the theory. - Right. Let's see. So, one member of the audience notes that the quest for immortality comes at the cost of increasing age and related diseases like
Alzheimer's, which is incurable. So, how do we deal with the trade-offs? - Yeah, so currently that is a big problem because people are living longer because we're tackling some diseases more successfully than others. For example, we're tackling diabetes
reasonably successfully, made tremendous progress with cancer and also with heart disease. But where we've been less
successful is with dementia. So as these people are living longer, they're at increased risk for dementia, which is another of the diseases of aging. And so, you could argue what's
the point of living longer if we're all going to get
Alzheimer's at the end? That's not a great way to end it all. But of course, one
thing you could argue is that the processes of aging, the fundamental processes of aging are similar in all tissue types. And so if we were to slow
down or tackle aging itself, you would also possibly
postpone diseases like dementia. So, that's the goal. But as I pointed out, there's
no real solid evidence that we'll be able to extend health without also extending life. So we may simply be
postponing all these things to later in life. - There's a question
about ribosome populations in various cancer tissues. And so there's an interest
in how do epigenetic changes affect the ribosome
populations in disease? - Even though I'm a ribosomologist, I'm afraid that's not
something I've looked into. Okay? It is true that cancer
cells need to divide, so they need more ribosomes, they need to make lots of protein, but I don't formally know the connection. - And we have one last question,
this is a great question because it's written in big block letters. (audience laughing) So the question is, is what
is the one piece of advice that you would have for us
to achieve healthy aging? - All right, so, when I... In my book, I end with the idea that, look, there are
interventions that we can do that currently work better than any anti-aging
therapy out on the market, like buying many of these supplements, and those are primarily this trio of diet, exercise, and sleep. Now, people have been
touting this for a long time, probably maybe even for centuries, but what we know now is how they work. We know what exercise does
to the biology of our cells, its regenerative capacity to
mitochondrial regeneration, so we know a lot of things
about what exercise does. Similarly, diet as related to those caloric restriction pathways. And so we know why diet can
have an effect on aging. And sleep, it turns out, isn't
just a sort of dormant state. Lots of things happen in our sleep cycle, like repair and recycling
of our components. And so, it turns out it's an
incredibly important aspect. And in fact, all species, even unicellular organisms
effectively sleep. They go through a daily cycle. And so those are three things,
and they help each other. For example, if you exercise, you're more likely to be relaxed and you're likely to sleep better. And if you're relaxed, you're
less likely to binge eat and so on. So, they sort of help each other. They also reduce stress. And stress is a driver of aging because of various direct
and indirect mechanisms. And so, it has broad
effects that are beneficial. Now, having said that, there
are two problems with it. And one is, these things also help with high blood pressure and cholesterol. So why am I on these pills? And it's because if I just
try to do it naturally, my blood pressure and cholesterol
still kept creeping up, so eventually I needed to take the pills. So you could say the goal of therapeutics is maybe to go beyond
what you can do yourself. But I think what you can do yourself should always be the first
line, first line of attack. - Okay, so it's diet, sleep, and exercise. That's the secret.
- Yeah. There are two social things
which are also correlated, which I found out in the
course of writing the book. One is that people who
are socially not isolated, they have a circle of friends and family, they tend to age more healthily
and also have longer lives, so lower mortality. The other is, and this is
of particular interest to me because I'm actually gonna
retire at the end of this month, and that is having a sense of
purpose lowers your mortality, independent of the social networking. And so I think... And that could take many forms; public service, volunteering,
maintaining interests, something that'll get you
out of bed every morning because you want to do something. So, I think those are
some additional factors. - That's good advice. So with that, I think we've come to the
end of our conversation, so I'd like to thank you, thank you Ramakrishnan for...
(audience applauding) - Thank you for having me. (audience applause continues) - And thank you to the
CUNY Graduate Center and the Cold Spring Harbor Lab
for sponsoring this lecture. (audience applauding) (people speaking indistinctly) (audio fades out)

Venkatraman also known as Venki Ramakrishnan it's indeed a great honor Venki to welcome you back to if I may say so your alma mater or your second alma mater Venki is an alum of this place he did his he had two choices at some point go to UCLA or come to UC San Diego and he looked at both places and hands-down he knew it was gonna be UC San Diego at least that's how I remember it you know being the Chancellor I do have a jaded view of life but that's how I remember it and maybe you can correct me but let me just say a few words about Venky he is an amazingly accomplished person he's done a lot in his life and I think it's just fair for me to read his bio verbatim so Venky is a senior scientist at the MRC Laboratory of Molecular Biology in Cambridge England and the president of the Royal his Bachelor of Science in physics at Baroda University in India in 1971 most of you would not have heard about it but and during a time when India's best and brightest were seeking opportunities not way the conversation went this afternoon he was looking to do something different and this I believe was in physics right the PhD was in physics and he knew at that time that biology was where the action was gonna be so he wanted to do something in biology okay so what following this achievement he switched his career path to life sciences beginning as a biology graduate student at UC San Diego I'd like to think that UC San Diego's non-traditional pioneering approach to all we do was uniquely suited to fostering Venky's non-traditional approach to his training as a physicist turned biologist and he left La Jolla after two years for postdoctoral fellowship in the department of chemistry at Yale and went on to advance his groundbreaking work on the ribosome at Brookhaven National Laboratory and the University of Utah and he received the Nobel Prize in Chemistry for his work on ribosome structure and function in 2009 and he is a knight of the British Empire an elected member of the National Academy of Sciences and the Indian National Academies and the Indian National Science Academy and the recipient of countless awards and honors he is indeed a beacon of inspiration not just to all of us out here but to a broader community that's doing research in this area across the world and so we are indeed very honored to have him back he's back here only for one day but I did offer him to come and spend a little bit more time with us next time so with that said welcome sir the podium is yours dr. Venki Ramakrishnan [Applause] so thank you very much it's a real pleasure to be back in La Jolla a place which really transformed my career and and my subsequent life so I'm here to talk today about a scientific memoir which I've called gene machine and it's available in two different forms as you can see as published last fall so there are four themes in the book the first is the the central character in the book is this amazing molecular machine that's one of the oldest molecules of life and it's the molecule that reads the code of life and really helps to build life build organisms and that includes everything from bacteria to all of us and a second thing is what it was like as an outsider I didn't go to until I came to UCSD I didn't go to really top-notch you know institutions certainly not top-notch research institutions and you know I was an immigrant to the u.s. at the age of 19 as you heard then I emigrated later in life to England and so what was it like to be an outsider who found himself in the middle of a high-stakes race for which which everyone knew was a really fundamental problem in biology and the other people in the race were all much better established groups much better connected as well a third thing is scientists often when they talk about their work they talk about it in highly sanitized terms you know everything in hindsight was very logical everybody was they are doing the right and Noble thing and so on the reality is it doesn't work that way because science is an enterprise is very pure you know as a scientific method and this hypothesis and you know evidence and but scientists are a different story altogether okay scientists are humans and so their personalities and ambitions and egos and rivalries and all sorts of things so and you throw prizes into the mix that's like adding gasoline to a fire okay and and then there are some lessons which are too late for me now but maybe they've there are young people in the audience they might you know get something from it so to get back to that the problem you know we've all heard of jeans you know if you talk to the average person and you mentioned jeans they're all nod their head you mentioned DNA they're not their head - they think they know what it means and what they really think is oh well genes are things that we have that sort of make us what we are you can have good genes or bad genes somebody's a mass murderer but they're bad genes somebody's genius to have good genes etc but the reality is if you guys if you probe people a little further and ask well what exactly is a gene they won't be able to tell you okay and when genes reside on a molecule called DNA and when people started sequencing all of our DNA you could get an estimate for how many genes we had and this is an old slide I really need to correct it because here it shows humans have a thirty-five to forty five thousand actually we only have about 25,000 genes and that's about the same number as a weed a rabbit hops italiana or you know only slightly more than a worm okay so anytime we think we're so much superior to other life-forms we're kind of kidding ourselves okay it's because we're fooled by our intelligence you know we think intelligence is what's important about life life doesn't care about intelligence life only cares about survival we care too we evolved intelligence you know in Africa to survive and that's how we you know drove our own evolution you know worm worms you know survive in different ways and bacteria not intelligent at all but they survived just fine so if you ask what genes are what are these 25,000 genes well each gene is a kind of a recipe on how to make a protein okay and so then you ask well what are proteins and why are they so important what proteins are carry out nearly all of the functions of life and so what are they they're they're long chains of building blocks chemical building blocks called amino acids and if you were to represent them as a chemical diagram this is what a protein would look like it's long chain and you can see these different groups sticking out and the different groups have different shapes and different chemical properties and there are about 20 of these in nature so every protein is a different combination of these 20 building blocks they're gonna be different sizes some of them are shorter some of them are longer and the order of the amino acids is different in each protein okay and you can represent it in a very schematic way so it's since there are 20 amino acids you can represent each amino acid by letters since we have 26 letters is more than enough to go around and you could just represent it as a string of letters and and that's what you know conceptually what a protein is now imagine if I told you I took a strip of paper or several strips of paper and I wrote down different sentences on these strips of paper and depending on what I wrote down each strip of paper magically folded up into a completely different shape okay that is exactly what happens with a protein so the information on how this chain folds up into three-dimensional shape is contained in the order of the letters the amino acids the building blocks that make up the protein okay and that's a kind of miracle I mean I would say I say miracle but really it's chemistry okay and so what do they fold up and what they look like well actually they can fold up into completely different shapes so for example this is three coils that are kind of wrapped around each other just like a braid you know and I grew up in India where women traditionally braid their hair and that's what it looks like and so you can have a three stranded kind of coil like that and that's collagen I some people may know about collagen injections and so on and you know collagen is what makes up our skin and connective tissue our cartilage and so on and actually it's the most abundant protein by weight in a human body and so it's what gives us our form without that would be kind of a blob a jello like blonde now on the bottom left is a protein and if you look at these dark objects they're called heme x' they're special groups that contain an iron atom and what this protein does is it captures oxygen in our lungs and transports it through our blood to the muscles and tissues where the oxygen is needed it's called hemoglobin it's one of the first proteins whose structure was solved and then this is a protein called rhodopsin it sits in the membrane of our eye of the cells in our eyes and it's what detects light and when it detects light it sends a series of signals which then eventually gets transmitted to the brain and that's how we actually can see something so what you can see is there here are three completely different proteins they have totally different shapes and yet every one of them was made somehow by decoding the instructions in our genes so how does that happen well genes reside on a molecule called DNA DNA is this long molecule which looks like a double helix and you can see they're 2 healers has wrapped around each other and just like proteins are built up of no acids which are the building blocks of proteins the building blocks of DNA are called bases and there are only four types of bases and one of the peculiarities is that each base will chemically pair So what's the reason behind this? Well, the reason is primarily evolutionary. So if you take a a small animal like a mouse uh which lives for two years, evolution has to choose how to use limited resources. So for example uh and traditionally for most species food and energy are limiting resources. And the question is, do you spend those resources on repair and maintenance or do you spend it on rapid growth, maturation, and reproduction? It only cares about fitness, evolutionary fitness, which is your ability to pass on your genes and uh produce viable offspring because it's the genes that are being uh selected for. And so in a mouse, the equation is to grow rapidly, produce lots of offspring fast, and then it doesn't matter if the individual dies because the genes have been passed on. And and that evolutionary theory is called antagonistic pleotropy. And it's the generally accepted theory uh by much of the aging community. V_y5P2Dxcmc.transcript:
Venki Ramakrishnan: The number of centenarians 
has been going up with time. But the number of   supercentenarians — people who live over 110 — 
is not going up at the same rate, and you would   expect it to also be going up. That suggests 
that we’re reaching some sort of hard limit. Supercentenarians are known for the fact 
that they’re extremely healthy and relatively   independent most of their lives; they don’t 
suffer from many of the diseases of old age   that most of us get. And then they have a rapid 
decline and die after they’ve reached 110. That   also suggests that there’s a sort of general 
breakdown of systems and things are collapsing. Now, this doesn’t mean that there’s a physical 
or chemical law that says we can’t live beyond   110 — because there are species, even 
vertebrates like whales and sharks,   that live several hundred years. What it means, 
though, is that in order to live beyond that,   we’re going to have to do something 
about the process of ageing itself. Luisa Rodriguez: Hi listeners. 
This is Luisa Rodriguez,   one of the hosts of The 80,000 Hours Podcast. In today’s episode, I was fortunate to 
speak with Venki Ramakrishnan — a Nobel   Prize–winning molecular biologist — who walked 
me through the cutting edge of anti-ageing   research. I found this incredibly valuable 
and interesting given that the longevity   field seems to be simultaneously full of 
genuinely mind-blowing scientific research,   and then also pretty full of 
baseless claims and false promises. Venki is great at breaking down complex topics in 
a way that a non-biologist can understand, so I   also came away with a way better understanding 
of the mechanics of ageing and death. Plus, toward the end, we have a fascinating 
talk about the social impacts of increasing   human lifespan — like what it would 
mean for our already-ageing population,   and why Venki thinks differential 
access to anti-ageing interventions   could actually make both economic 
and health inequality loads worse. All right, without further ado, 
I bring you Venki Ramakrishnan. Today I’m speaking with Venki Ramakrishnan 
— molecular biologist, Nobel Prize winner,   and author of Why We Die: The New Science of Aging   and the Quest for Immortality. Thanks 
for coming on the podcast, Venki. Venki Ramakrishnan: Thank you. It’s a pleasure. Luisa Rodriguez: So I hope to talk about 
which ageing and longevity research is   actually promising and which is overhyped. 
But first, why did you write Why We Die? Venki Ramakrishnan: I think for several 
reasons. First is it’s a big existential   question. We may be the only species that’s 
aware of mortality. Other species are aware   of death — they’ll mourn one of their own when 
it has died — but we’re the only species that   probably realises that we have an expiration 
date, that we are unlikely to live past a   certain age. That awareness of mortality 
has really driven a lot of human culture;   a lot of human culture is about denying death, 
and thinking of it as a passage to something else. And for most of our existence as a species,   there’s really not much we could do about 
it — that’s why people say “as certain as   death and taxes.” But in the last few decades, 
molecular biology has made tremendous advances,   and we’re beginning to understand the real 
underlying causes of ageing, which leads to death. Now, coupled with that, what’s happening is that 
societies are getting older. People are living   longer, fertility rates are dropping — and this 
means that the population as a whole is getting   older, there’s a bigger fraction at the older 
age of the spectrum. And that means that we   really need to ensure that, as people age, they 
continue to be healthy, preferably independent,   and preferably productive. That’s leading to 
a lot of investment in anti-ageing research   to understand what happens with age 
and how to combat those effects. Along with support from governments 
and various funding agencies,   there’s also been a huge explosion 
of the anti-ageing industry — about   700 longevity companies in just the last 20 
years alone, and tens of billions of dollars   gone into the industry — and that itself 
has also generated a huge amount of hype. So I am a molecular biologist, and I 
work in the area of how proteins are   made and how protein synthesis is regulated and 
controlled. It turns out that those processes   are very central to ageing: during ageing, 
our control of protein synthesis starts going   awry. So I’m close to the field, but I don’t 
actually work in the field of ageing per se.   I thought somebody who’s very close to the 
field, but not in it, and I don’t have any   vested interests, this is a good time to
