called the epigenetics markers on the DNA back to uh zero and letting it start over again of course that can only buy you it certainly buys you a certain amount of time I'm not sure it's going to buy you a thousand years you know you see what I mean there's going to be gradual uh damage and there's also a huge amount of selection these clone uh cloning procedures are high highly selective most of them uh turn out to be defective at either the implantation or the embryo stage and are never make it as as full-time uh animals now what was the other question right that the the lady on the same row had a sort of semi-related question she was talking about the very long lived animals so and and that's that's a great question what is it about their cellular mechanism so there is live that long yeah there's a a a paradox called Pito Paradox after the person who uh you know identified it which is if you have a long large animal it has many more cells and therefore even if one of them becomes cancerous they ought to get cancer uh you know more they're more like should be more likely to get cancer but in fact they don't and it turns out that very large animals uh have repair Pathways for example DNA uh damaged repair Pathways uh that are are perhaps better at maintenance and repair uh than smaller animals they also have slower metabolism and that also is related it's if you have a faster metabolism you're more likely uh to have a shorter lifespan it's inversely related so the Greenland shark can barely move in as extremely slow it has um you know and its body temperature is quite low so and same with the galapagus: uh protein which is involved in the DNA damage response called p-53 it turns out elephants have something like 20 copies of it you know compared to a mouse which might only have one excellent now about menopause menopause is a very puzzling phenomenon the idea that it occurs at a point and then uh you know our species lives long after uh menopause and it might it it seemed to be something of a puzzle and there all kinds of social explanations uh postulated for menopause by the way only a few species that we know of undergo true menopause one of them are killer whales and uh even elephants although they elephants fertility declines with age it's not zero it's not this sharp you know cut off uh that we that we have in humans and uh so one idea is that um original idea was called the mother hypothesis which is that you need to survive long enough to bring up your Offspring until they are independent and can survive on their own and become mature and are able to reproduce themselves because if you die before that then you know the chances are they'll die and you won't be able to pass on your genes won't be passed on and since humans have such a long period of childhood and dependency you have to live that much longer after your last child so that's that was one Theory okay and of course in the past you know most of us didn't live much past menopause age we you know most humans died around 35 or 40 and it's only a few that survived uh much beyond that the other idea was called is called the grandmother hypothesis which is is that it's more advantageous for a woman to spend resources on raising helping raise their grandchildren in the you know her own or or or in the family uh compared to producing more children of her own there's also risk with childbirth as you get older so that's another idea but people argue it's never better to invest in people who have only a quarter of your genes when you could produce Offspring which have a half your genes and so evolutionarily do you you're not a fan of the I I'm not a fan of the grandmother hypothesis I think it's evolutionarily it doesn't make sense but one idea is that these are all what are called adaptive theories they're theories that it somehow benefits the group and evolutionary biologists generally don't like these things that benefit the group unless of course the group is all related highly related genetically like bees in a colony uh then it does make mathematical sense but otherwise it does does any such Theory would would result in genes that are unstable but the other idea is that we only started Living much longer about 40,000 years ago and so the number of eggs and age of menopause was really matched our life expectancy 40,000 years ago and it simply hasn't had time to adjust to our our longer uh lifespan and that that strikes me as a more reasonable uh explanation excellent I think we have a question or two from online yes we do yeah we have one question uh coming in here from Kish makam uh what's your opinion about res veratrol as an anti-aging agent a lot of controversy about that in the scienic community the ingredient that was in wine this is yes the ingredient in red wine and you know all these inop files were uh you know Jo you know ecstatic when it was shown that resveratol uh would increase um the thing but actually Resveratrol was thought to be a ceran uh activator and uh it turns out what is this in activator sorry about that s so people did experiments in yeast after the discovery that you could get mutations and worms that double their lifespan uh somebody named Leonard guarente at MIT uh did experiments in yeast and isolated mutations which map to A protein that uh effectively added a uh well removed an acetal group removed a chemical group from uh molecules and in particular it might have removed them from uh molecules that were involved in whether genes would be active or not active so it was thought that maybe it's controlling some sort of the way in which cells Express genes and that's how it might exert its effect on longevity and then people started looking for compounds that would activate these molecules and it turned out that um that one of them was identified to be resveratol which is an ingredient in red wine and also in other uh compounds I believe berries and things like that so this uh caused a flurry of excitement and uh you know people founded biotech companies that were sold to Glo welcome for 750 million dollars and I'm sure some of the early investors were very happy but Glo uh 20 years down the line really didn't come up with anything and eventually closed down the division that um you know was dealing with this and subsequently uh lots of experiments have cast doubt on that whole idea and uh I think most aging Specialists that I've talked to uh don't really subscribe to it but I should say uh the original discoverers still uh you you know hang on you know are convinced that their uh ideas are correct and they're not uh willing to concede and this reminds me of uh Max Plank's dictum Max plank was a famous physicist and he said you know ideas don't change because the people who formulated a theory uh are convinced that their theory was wrong and and you know agree with some New Concept rather they just die and then a new generation comes along that just simply ignores uh what they've found and and you could put this as science Marches On One funeral at a time and so uh that's uh that's how it is but but of course you know the people who believe in in the siru uh you know related uh aging uh causes uh still stand by it I should say I think that's a really good point at which to which to end thank you very much for all the questions online and in the room but most of all uh our thanks to you Veni for an incredible talk and discussion conversation today it's been amazing thank [Applause] you thanks to our speakers tonight and for the wonderful chairing by Angela please do come outside and check out the bookstore as well and to and safe journey home thank you and and have your glass of red wine [Applause] [Music]

WquA6pv1OgY.title: WquA6pv1OgY
WquA6pv1OgY.transcript:
Good evening, everyone. Thank you so much for 
joining us tonight. My name is Kate Bruns, and on   behalf of Harvard Book Store, the Harvard University 
Division of Science and the Cabot Science Library,   I am very pleased to welcome you to our program 
tonight with Venki Ramakrishnan discussing his   latest book, "Gene Machine: the Race to Decipher the 
Secrets of the Ribosome". In just a moment, I'm going   to turn you over to Professor Stephen Harrison 
of the Department of Biological Chemistry and   Molecular Pharmacology at Harvard Medical School, 
who's going to introduce the program this evening.   But first I'd just like to take a quick moment 
to tell you about this science book talk series -  a collaboration between the Harvard Division of 
Science, the Cabot Science Library and Harvard   Book Store. The series features talks throughout 
the academic year by the authors of recently-   published books on a wide variety of science- 
related topics. Next up on June 5th, we will   welcome the acclaimed author of "The Strangest 
Man",  Dr. Graham Farmelo, for a discussion of   his latest book, "The Universe Speaks in Numbers: 
How Modern Math Reveals Nature's Deepest Secrets".   He will be joined in conversation by Harvard 
Physics' very own Jacob Barandes. To learn   more about the Science Book Talks and to learn 
about Harvard Book Store's many other upcoming   events, you can visit us online at Harvard.com and 
sign up for our weekly email newsletter. Tonight's   talk is going to be followed by some time for 
your questions, after which we will have a book   signing and refreshments in the Cabot Science 
Library - just outside this room and across the   Science Center entryway. And now I will turn 
things over to Professor Stephen Harrison. --So as the size of the audience suggests, and as 
the topic of the book which is really in part at   least an autobiographical memoir indicates, 
I won't spend any time introducing Venki   Ramakrishnan at all except to comment for 30 
seconds on the subtitle: "the race to decipher   the secrets of the ribosome". Scientific races - and 
you've probably read or heard about some of them -  like any race, tend to have a defined goal line, 
but there are lots of ways of approaching that   goal line or even in fact in defining it in truly 
scientific terms. And so some people get there, if   you wish, by being very good at organizing the 
right kind of team, and other people by knowing   very deeply what the right kind of question to 
ask is, and then figuring out how to get there.   Venki is certainly in the latter category -
a very deep and very fine scholar - and that's   characterized, I believe, specially his role in 
this race to decipher things. He's also a very   felicitous prose stylist as those of you who 
have read, are reading or will read his book,   which I heartily recommend, and he's also now 
unusual among scientists - taken on a serious   task in public service as president of the Royal 
Society - but you've come here to hear him so - thank you. --Thank you very much, Steve, for that very kind 
introduction. So I'm going to start off - I have - I   want to try and finish on time so I'm going to not 
waste too much time, but I will say I've noticed a   lot of people who I know are biologists in the 
audience, and my lecture is really not meant for   biologists, it's meant for the general public - 
but I take heart from Enrico Fermi who said   "Never under - underestimate the pleasure you give 
people by telling them what they already know" so   without further ado, I will plunge into the book. 
Now this book has four themes. One is about the   ribosome itself, which is this amazingly complex 
and ancient machine that reads the code of life   to make you and me and every other life form. 
The other is a more personal story which is   interwoven - which is my life as an outsider and 
what it's suddenly found - felt like to suddenly   be in the middle of a high-stakes race or even 
in the pursuit of an important problem. A third   is about the human messiness of science. Often 
science books - there are some notable exceptions,   including from a former Harvard faculty member - 
but by and large, science books sanitize what   science is like. In hindsight, everything is logical, 
everybody's altruistic, it's a noble pursuit for   the truth, etcetera, but in reality science is done by 
humans, with all of the problems that humans have -   egos, personalities, rivalry, question of competition 
versus collaboration and how prizes throw a sort   of- you know -combustible element into this already 
somewhat complicated mix. And then finally I might   end with one slide on lessons from own life in 
science which are far too late for me to - you   know, learn from - but which perhaps young people 
in the audience might find something from... so I   want to start off by asking the question of what 
is a gene? If you asked the - you know - if you walk   down outside, even around Harvard Square, and ask 
people 'what is a ribosome?' most people won't have   the slightest idea what a ribosome is. If you ask 
some 'what is a gene?' most people say 'oh yeah, I know   what a gene is: it's that thing that gives us - you 
know - traits, our characters, we - and we have good   genes and bad genes that we inherit from parents 
and so on, but if you really ask them 'well, what is   a gene?' they won't be able to tell you, okay? And 
they'll be particularly surprised to find that   humans have actually this - this is an over-estimate 
from 2001, but we have only about 25,000 genes, and   we don't have any more genes than a weed called 
Arabidopsis thaliana or a worm or called C. elegans -   you know - so anytime we think - we're so 
much more, you know, at the pinnacle of life, it -  you know - we should take some - learn some humility 
by - from this fact and reali - the reality is life,   it does not select for intelligence, it selects for 
survivability - and you know, intelligence is a trait   that helped us survive in certain circumstances; it 
may in fact lead to our extinction for all we know...   but - anyway -  so we have thousands of genes - and 
even bacteria have thousands of genes - and so the   question is what are genes? And what genes are are 
essentially long chains made up of building blocks   called amino acids and there are about 20 types 
of amino acids, so you could in principle represent   a protein chain by a string of letters. Now these 
amino acids, each one of them, they have different   properties: some of them don't like water, some of 
them are charged, some of them are neutral and so   on - so they have - they're capable of very different 
chemical properties and as a result, they - the   order of the amino acids that make up a protein 
contain in them the information on how to fold   up the protein into a particular three-dimensional 
structure. Now pause and think about how remarkable   this is: it would be as if I took several strips 
of paper and wrote down a different sentence of   a different length on each strip of paper, and 
depending on what I wrote this - the strip of paper -   magically folded, it - it's folded itself up into a 
particular three-dimensional shape. That is how amazing   it is - except it's not a miracle, it's chemistry, and 
has to do with the way that amino acids interact   with each other. And just to give you an example of 
the variety of shapes and functions proteins can   have, here is a protein which is a long filament- 
like protein which consists of three strands that   are woven around each other and it's called 
collagen, and it's what makes up your skin and   cartilage and connective tissue. In fact, it's 
the largest - it's the most abundant protein by   weight in us: this is a protein called hemoglobin, 
which captures oxygen in our lungs and transports   the oxygen through our blood to the tissues and 
muscles where it's needed. And this is a protein   called rhodopsin, which is present in the membrane 
of cells in your retina and detects light and   allows you to see. So these are just three examples 
from the thousands of proteins that we have and -   you know, these proteins carry out all of those 
thousands of functions. Now you might ask "well, okay,   genes contain information on how to make proteins" 
 -I've just told you what proteins are like - "how do   genes actually contain that information and how 
is it all - how does - how does that work?" Well, genes   reside - with some exceptions, like viruses somewhere - 
some viruses - it resides in a molecule which is   familiar to all of you, which is DNA, and DNA, as you 
know, consists of two strands, and instead of being   made of twenty types of building blocks DNA is 
made of four types of building blocks, called bases.   And the remarkable thing about the DNA structure 
was that the structure showed that each of the   four base pairs has a particular partner among 
the other three that it pairs with - so A will   only pair with a T, and C will only pair with 
a G and vice-versa. And what this meant is that   if you have a particular order of bases on one 
strand, that would specify the order of bases on   the other strand because - because of the way they 
would pair up. And so if you separated the strands   each one contained the information it made - you 
needed to make the other strand. And this is how   the problem, a centuries-old problem of how do you 
get two cells, or two of anything, from one was - was   solved in molecular terms but what the structure 
didn't tell you was how you could somehow from   the order of the four bases  -- figure out how to 
make a protein, which consists of 20 types of   amino acids. And this took almost a decade after 
the structure was solved - actually a little over   a decade - and it turns out that what happens is: in 
a long DNA molecule, different stretches represent   genes that code for different proteins. So for 
example, we have 23 chromosomes and we have about   20,000 genes, so you could see that each chromosome 
on average might contain a thousand genes, and so   they're sort of spread out over -you know - different 
parts of the molecule, and what the cell does is --   now if you were to go to the Library of Congress, 
or where I live to the British Library, and say 'I   want to read a particular book' - they're not going 
to let you read that book - it's too valuable - what   they'll say is 'we'll give you a copy of the book 
to read and you can take it with you and you can   read it'. And the cell does something like that in 
that you can think of DNA and our genome as the   library of all of the information of genetic 
information, and then it makes a copy of each   gene as it needs it in order to be read to make a 
particular protein, and that copy is a very similar   molecule to DNA called RNA and then - which also 
consists of four types of bases and the order of   bases is exactly what it would be like on one of 
the strands of DNA where - which contains genetic   information. Somehow from there you have to get 
to this totally different type of chain which   consists of twenty types of amino acids. Now of 
course if each base specified an amino acid you   could only code for four amino acids and even if 
you read them two at a time you could code for   four times four or 16, so not surprisingly nature 
reads these bases three at a time and they're   called - units called "codons" because it's the unit 
of the genetic code. And very early on Francis   Crick realized that amino acids wouldn't be able 
to recognize these codons directly so there must   be some sort of adapter molecule, and that adapter 
molecule is another kind of RNA molecule called   tRNA  -T is for transfer, because it transfers 
amino acids to a growing protein chain and at one   end it has three bases that read the three bases 
by matching - by pairing - in the - in the gene and at   the other end, it brings along the appropriate 
amino acid. And so for a different codon you   would have a different tRNA molecule that would 
bring in a different amino acid and somehow these   amino acids then have to be stitched together to 
make a protein. And of course this doesn't happen   by itself: it is - it's a highly complicated and 
ordered process -and the first hint of what might   be happening was when cell biologists, notably 
George Palade, showed that newly-made proteins   occurred in these blobs, in these organelles called 
endoplasmic reticulum in our cells, and when these   blobs were isolated it was found out that they 
were all about the same size but if you look   closely there's a divider in each particle. And 
what it meant was that each particle actually   consisted of two pieces called a large and a small 
subunit, and these particles were isolated from a   fragment of the cell but you break open the cell 
and you just fractionate different parts of the   cell, and the fraction from which these particles 
were isolated was called microsomes - which are   really fragments of the endoplasmic reticulum - and 
when they analyzed these particles they found out   they were about two-thirds RNA - which stands for 
ribonucleic acid - and one-third protein, so they   were originally called "the ribonucleoprotein 
particles of the end microsomal fraction". Now   of course, you know, if you wanted to talk about it 
this was rather inconvenient, and so Howard [Dinsis?]  at some meeting in the 50s said 'Look, why don't 
we just call it the ribosome?' and that's the name   that's stuck ever since. People went on to 
analyze the ribosome and they found out it was   incredibly complex: it's - even in bacteria which 
have the simpler version, it has about 50 proteins   and three large pieces of RNA distributed among 
the two subunits that together make up the entire   ribosome. And then people figured out - through very 
clever biochemistry - a worldwide community working   for 10 or 20 years figured out that the ribosome 
has three slots for binding those adaptor tRNA   molecules. The small subunit binds the mRNA which 
contains the genetic information, and the ribosome   figures out where to start making the protein by 
where to start reading the genetic information to make   proteins and brings along the first tRNA with the 
first amino acid; and then in - in the second slot,   it selects just the right tRNA that corresponds to 
the three bases on the gene, so it's the gene that's   specifying which tRNA comes in and therefore which 
amino acid is brought to the ribosome and then - the   ribosome, if it's the correct one, accepts it and 
then stitches together a bond between the first   and second amino acid and then the whole assembly 
moves. The old tRNA is kicked out and then a third   tRNA comes in - and only if it's the right tRNA 
is it accepted and the ribosome then stitches   a bond between the second and third amino acid 
so you see what's happening: it's choosing tRNAs   depending on what the code is specifying on the 
gene, and then at the same time it's stitching   together the amino acids brought in by those tRNA 
molecules - and this is how genetic information is   used to build a protein in exactly the right 
order specified by the information in the gene.   Now all this was known when I first entered the 
ribosome. Now the book is organized as a sort of   interweaving of science and my own personal story, 
so I'm using my personal story as to give it a   narrative structure and dribbling in the science 
as I go. And so I start off by pointing out that   at the age of 19, I graduated with a degree in 
physics from my hometown university, in Baroda,   India, and went off to Ohio University - in Athens in 
southeastern Ohio - to get a Ph.D, and this is where I   spent the first five years of my life, and I should 
say my most notable accomplishment perhaps my only   notable accomplishment during those five years is 
that I met and married my wife. And you might ask   well - how is - what happened? Well, here's the problem: I 
started doing physics, I took all my coursework,I passed my   comprehensive exams - all of that was fine, it was no 
problem. Then I started doing research and this is   what I found: I found it was actually very hard 
to actually make really original contributions   in physics, okay? And one of the problems is that 
physics - it's a very mature discipline, and you know   there'd been very few what I would call really big 
breakthroughs even in the few decades when I was a -   previous few decades when I'd been a graduate 
student and I thought if I stayed on in physics   this is what I would be doing - I would be doing 
boring calculations that- you know - didn't actually   improve understanding but that might - you know - I 
could publish, maybe even get tenure possibly, but   you know - it wouldn't actually really be advancing 
physics. I felt I didn't have that sort of aptitude   or- or talent, and then I looked at biology. I used 
to read Scientific American and I realized that   every issue of Scientific American in the scient -
in the 70s, and even probably today, contained major   breakthroughs in biology. You know that was a time 
when DNA's was - people had first figured out how   to sequence DNA - Some of you may know Wally Gilbert 
who was here - who's here at Harvard, and you know   the structure of molecules inside cells, even the 
organization of cells, immunology, all of this stuff   was being - you know - broken wide open and I knew 
that many physicists had made this transition   including people like Max Delbrück and Max Perutz, 
Francis Crick, so I knew it was possible and I had   written to a number of people - universities - to 
ask whether I could go to graduate school again   in biology - but you know at Yale, they said 'we can't 
take you as a graduate student. but we - we - some of   our faculty might offer you a postdoc and one of 
the people who offered me a postdoc, oddly enough,   was Tom Steitz, with whom I ended up sharing the 
Nobel Prize. Another was Don Engelman. And I turned   them both down, saying 'I don't know any biology, 
and I really need to go to graduate school' and   so I went off to University of California/San 
Diego, which accepted me into their graduate   program with the idea that I might do a second Ph.D. 
And so I had by this time I had gotten married: I   had a six-year-old stepdaughter and a four-week 
old son and a very tolerant wife who didn't mind   my going back to graduate school with a four- 
week old kid and a six-year-old stepdaughter   in tow and we packed our stuff in a Ryder truck 
and -you know - drove off to California which is   where I spent two years - and the thing I remember 
most about biology is that in the first week, when   professors would talk about their research 
to sort of introduce new graduate students,   I couldn't understand a thing of what they were 
saying and I realized that I needed to go back   to undergraduate classes. So I spent the first year 
taking undergraduate biochemistry, cell biology and   genetics, and I had a Ph.D in physics, and I was competing with all these cutthroat pre-meds so - so   there we were. So by the end of that my set - [?]
sometime during my second year, I felt I'd learned   enough biology. I'd learned how to do experiments 
and, you know, do the sort of things that biol-  but are   involved by a chemical research and I thought, 
maybe I don't really need to get a second Ph.D,   and around this time I read another article in 
Scientific American and one of the authors was   Don Engelman who had offered me a postdoc and so I 
wrote to him and I said, 'Look, you wanted to offer   me a postdoc when I didn't know a damn thing in 
biology and now I've actually learned some, so   maybe you're still interested,' and so he put me 
in touch with his collaborator Peter Moore, who   interviewed me and offered me a postdoc, and so 
there we are two years later, crisscrossing the   country again to go to New Haven where I worked 
for this man - he is Peter Moore -he has a somewhat   glowering expression in this picture but he's 
actually a very nice person - and we were trying   to understand where the different proteins were in 
the small subunit of the ribosome. There are about   20 of them - nobody even knew where they were on the 
ribosome - and it used a somewhat esoteric technique   called "neutron scattering". And I like to joke that 
a third of these balls are mine - but anyway, by the   end of this I applied for a number of faculty 
jobs and actually about 50 distributed between   research universities and four-year colleges and 
I got exactly zero interviews, and I - you can sort   of see why - you know - they - here I was working on a 
problem that was already considered old-fashioned   and using a technique that nobody had heard of in 
a biology department - and you know - I had this sort   of weird track record and the four-year 
colleges probably thought, you know, 'this guy has   this long unpronounceable name, we don't even know 
if he can speak English, you know, let alone teach,   okay?'  So I went into pile B in both cases and I 
ended up going to Brookhaven National Lab where   I continued to do what -this neutron scattering 
for things- but the real problem was it was sort   of a dead end because it wasn't getting us any 
closer to understanding how the ribosome would   work, and to understand what - what I mean by that: 
one important thing in science is that every   time you've been able to see things, visualize 
things at a different level of detail, it has   dramatically transformed science. I'll give you 
just a couple of examples in physics: the ability   to see planets and their moons using a telescope -
Galileo really transformed our -you know- view of   physics and astronomy. And another example is 
in biology - the ability to see things that are   microscopic. Von Leeuwenhoek and Robert Hooke 
led to the discovery of microbes and bacteria,   and also led to the idea that living things are 
organized in units called cells. Both of these   were completely transformational, and the same was 
true of molecules. You know - if you want to seal   something you've got on if you want to understand 
something, you've got to understand what it looks   like. And I'd give you the example of a car.  
If you just look at it from - you know- outer   space, it looks just like a dot moving around - you 
get closer, you start seeing it's got lights and   wheels - still doesn't tell you how car works. If you 
open it up, you start understanding how a car might   be put together, but you don't understand how it 
works. It's only when you know the details of what   a car actually looks like how and what and how 
an engine is put together how it's connected to -   through a crankshaft, etcetera, that you understand how 
a car might work. And it's the same with molecules.   Now if you want to look at small objects - what 
you would normally do is you would take an object   and have a magnifying glass or a microscope, 
and you would then obtain a magnified image,   and that - that would allow you to see detail that 
you couldn't see with the naked eye. Now what a  lens does is it recombines scattered rays from 
the object and then produces an image by recom-   recombining them, and depending on the geometry, 
the image can be much larger and so you can see   detail. But the thing you have to keep in mind 
is the lens does not produce the information:   it's simply using the information  - so in other 
words, if there were no lens here, the scattered   rays here would still have all of the information 
you needed to make that image. The lens is simply   recombining them. And - now - if you wanted to look at 
molecules, though, you couldn't do it this way, and   the reason is that the distance between atoms in 
a molecule is several thousand times smaller than   the wavelength of light. There's a very old law 
in physics from the 19th century that you can't   distinguish two objects that are closer together 
than about half the wavelength of the light you're   using. So how would you look at molecules? Well, this 
was discovered by Lawrence Bragg, who you know did   this when he was a graduate student, and went on to 
become the youngest-ever Nobel laureate in science -   still is the youngest Nobel laureate in science 
at the age of 25 - and that is you can take molec - molecules and persuade them to stack up in very 
regular three-dimensional arrays and what they   then form are what we call crystals: that's what 
crystals are - the regular three-dimensional arrays   of something - from a molecular object - and so here 
is a picture of crystals and what you would then   do is hit them with X-rays. Now X-rays, it turns out, 
do have the right sort of wavelength to be able to   see detail, but there aren't any good lenses to 
focus X-rays and produce an image as you would   with light and even if there were, by the time 
you hit it with enough X-rays to see an image   you would end up frying the molecule. So the way 
you do it is you hit crystals with x-rays and that   amplifies the signal from millions of molecules 
which are all sitting in the same orientation, and   then you measure the scattered rays and this is 
what the scattered rays would look like - because   it's a crystal, the rays are scattered in long 
certain directions and I try to explain that in   the book - and then you would do in a computer 
what a lens actually does which is you would   recombine these rays - the information in these 
rays - to calculate the three-dimensional image   of the molecule, okay? And that's how you know this 
technique called crystallography works - and that's   how, you know, for over a hundred years molecular 
structures have been directly visualized. Now I   didn't know any crystallography and I was coming 
up for tenure at Brookhaven National Lab, and they   asked me 'If you -you know, if we give you tenure 
what will you do?' and I said 'well, if you give   me tenure, the first thing I'll do is stop what 
I'm doing which seems to be a bit dead-end - and   go on sabbatical to learn crystallography.' So they 
were somewhat taken aback by my honesty but - but to   their credit they let me do that -  and gave me 
tenure, okay? And so I decided to go to the - a very   famous lab called the MRC Lab of Molecular Biology, 
where the some of the first protein structures had   been determined, and the person who was director when 
I went there was Aaron Klug who was working - I was   doing some work in his area and I had actually 
crystallized a protein that was of interest to   him, so that was the sort of bait that I used 
to see if he would bite, and luckily he didn't   throw my letter in the garbage can. He sponsored 
me for a Guggenheim Fellowship and I went there   and learned crystallography and when I came back I 
collaborated with a colleague of mine, Steve White,   and what we did was we took each protein from 
the ribosome, tried to crystallize it, and then   solve its structure - so instead of balls we had 
a detailed picture of each protein. But we had no   idea where they were in the ribosome, how they fit 
into the ribosome and of course, the two-thirds of   the ribosome that was RNA was completely - you 
know - a black box to us, so after a while this   sort of thing seemed like stamp collecting we 
were getting nowhere with understanding how a   ribosome would work. It would be a bit like 
going to an automobile parts store: you've   never seen what a car looks like and then just 
looking at different parts and saying 'oh, isn't   that interesting?' - but you have no idea what it does, 
or where it fits in the car, okay? So how - what was   the way forward? Well, the way forward was started 
when Ada Yonath, who's an Israeli scientist went   to Heinz-Günter Wittmann's lab in Berlin. He was 
a director of a very big lab in Berlin and he was   a very famous ribosomologist, and together they 
obtained the first crystals of the large subunit   of the ribosome and this actually turned out to be 
a big feat because it was about - almost an order of   magnitude about five times larger than the largest 
thing that had been crystallized up to that point,   apart from very regular objects like viruses. So 
we had crystals of the large subunit, and then   a group in Russia headed by Maria Garber used a 
different species to obtain crystals of both the   small subunit and the entire ribosome, so now we 
had crystals of both subunits of the ribosome and the entire ribosome, but then the field was sort 
of stuck for many years. And so in the mid-1990s,   even 15 years after the first crystals, the work - 
there was no apparent progress towards a structure,   not even a sort of what you call a fuzzy or low- 
resolution structure, okay? and there had been - Ada  Yonath had spent many years to try and improve 
crystals of the large subunit, but even several   years after those much-improved crystals had been 
reported, there'd been no progress towards, say, any   kind of structure - not even an intermediate 
structure and so after two meetings when it   really seemed as though the field was stuck, other 
groups decided that then he probably needed to get   in there and tackle the car -you know, the field -
and perhaps use different approaches and so the   Yale group decided consisting of my mentor Peter 
Moore and Tom Staitz, who had at one point offered   me a postdoc as I mentioned, decided to use the 
crystals that Ada Yonath had discovered in her lab,   and decided to see how far they could get with 
it. Now, I thought, 'well that's fine - let them fight   it out with the 50S subunit' - and I thought that it 
might be a better approach to try and improve one   of the other things. There were no good crystals of 
the small subunit, or of the entire ribosome, and so   I thought, 'well the whole ribosome was really 
too complicated and might come later' and so I   thought 'well, let's tackle the small subunit - it's 
small only in relative to the large subunit but   actually it was a huge problem - is - it's almost 1 
million daltons in molecular weight and had, you know,    150 thousand atoms, so there's an enormously 
complex structure and I was - I had just moved   to the University of Utah at that time and I had 
two students, Bill Clements and John McCutcheon and   they were both naive first-year graduate students, 
and I told them that - you know - 'how about working on   the structure of the 30S subunit of the ribosome?' 
and since they didn't know anything about either   ribosomes or crystallography, they thought it was 
a great idea for a Ph.D project and so that's a -   so we were off - and this guy, Brian Wimberly, was 
very very knowledgeable about RNA structure and -   and all sorts of things. He was a postdoc; he was 
actually on his second postdoc - and he knew too   much - so he didn't want to start -you know - working 
on the project right away, but as soon as we had   hints it was going to take off he was - luckily 
for me - he was happy to join the project. And this   is me, in my "bin Laden" days, and you can - you can 
see from this photograph that I'm the only guy   who's wearing gloves when this picture was taken, 
and showing that I was the only guy was actually   working when his picture was taken, okay? So we 
started off, we got good crystals, and then I went   to a meeting in Sweden where the Yale group 
clearly showed that they were making progress   on the 50S subunit. Ada Yonath showed data 
which she believed meant that the 50S subunit was   impossible to solve and she said 'it's not worth 
tackling - it's an unfeasible problem' and then - but   then - which I was perfectly relaxed about, it had 
nothing to do with me, but then to my surprise, she   showed pictures of the small subunit which now 
had improved diffraction so instead of the Yale   group and Ada duking it out on the 50S, the Yale 
group was now going to have a free ride and I was   going to be in a head-to-head competition that I 
had never wanted to with Ada Yonath and she had at   the time really three labs: she had a Max Planck 
out- station in Hamburg; she had a biochemical Max   Planck support group in Berlin, and she had her lab 
in Weizmann with 15 or 20 people and I had my two   graduate students in - in Utah - and so on the plane 
ride home from Sweden, I almost thought I ought to   give up. I mean, I thought 'what is the point?' And 
then I realized two things: one is I thought - I   thought crystals of these things have been around 
for 15 years - nobody's actually figured out how   to crack the problem and just because there's 
another crystal form now, it doesn't mean that   she's suddenly going to - you know, solve it - and - 
and so I thought, 'well that's one thing' and the   other thing I thought was 'if I just stand back 
and watch other people do this, I'll be kicking   myself for the rest of my life - this is the most 
important problem in my field and it's a central   problem in biology; and on the other hand, if I 
do a plunge ahead and give it my best shot, if   it doesn't work out I feel that I gave it my best 
shot' so I plunged ahead. And around this time [Harry   Noller?] had personal reasons to visit Utah because 
he was in a relationship with a colleague of mine -   Brenda Bass - at the time and - and at one of these - 
one of his visits, he said, 'You know I'm working   on structure - of crystal structure of the entire 
ribosome' and I thought, how could Harry, who's a   biochemist who knows nothing about crystallography 
be working on the structure of the entire ribosome?    - and I realized what had happened was he had 
hired this guy, Marat Yusupovs - you might have   recognized, was a guy at the top right in that 
Russian group - and so Marat and his wife [Gonara?] were part of the original Russian group that 
had crystallized, you know, the whole ribosome,   and he had hired Jamie Cate who had solved one 
of the largest RNA structures at the time which   was the Group I Intron Structure - so Harry had 
assembled a crack team with just the right kind   of expertise. So suddenly, my quiet niche 
had become a kind of four-way race. Now the thing   you don't do - what is the thing that you must not 
do in the middle of a a tight race?  - and the thing   you must not do is lose time and what I instead 
did was to move to a different continent, okay? - in   the middle of a four-way race, and so the question 
is why did I do this? We may remember I had spent   a sabbatical year at the MRC Lab of Molecular 
Biology and I really liked the way the lab was   run - and I thought it would be the ideal place to 
do this sort of work. In fact, there were two people   I asked and one of them - for advice, and one of them 
actually was Steve Harrison, who introduced me, and   the reason was I wanted to work on whole ribosome 
structure. And although the Yale group seemed to   be making some progress I had no idea how long it 
would take - and you know University labs are funded   on NIH grants which last for four years or five 
years and so at the end of year three or four, if   you haven't made your breakthrough and published 
a bunch of papers, they're not going to renew it   and if you just tell them, 'well, you know there's 
this group that it's been -  have had crystals for   15 years with a large number of people but you 
know, it's okay, I have really good ideas for how to   get crystals and how to solve it' - you know, you can 
just sort of imagine what a panel might - how a panel   might view that; actually, I have a view they would 
probably laugh and throw my application in the bin -   and so I needed a place that had stable funding. 
And I also thought it would be a good - to go to a   place which had really expert crystallographic 
colleagues, because this was a problem that was   pushing the limits of the technology, of the 
technique to beyond what had actually been done up   to that point, and they beyond that had a very collegial 
atmosphere so people would be willing to help you   and talk to you about your problems and it's also 
an institution that had a history of supporting   important problems for the prolonged period. It has 
had a series of Nobel Prizes, including the last   two years, but if you look at the last two years, 
the prize was awarded for things that were started   25 or 30 or 40 years ago, okay? - and so you know it 
does have that sort of patient view of things. So   I persuaded my very tolerant wife that we should 
take a fifty percent or so pay cut - actually   40 percent but given the cost of living was more 
like 50 percent pay cut - and moved to England,   away from our children who are now grown up and 
luckily for me, she said yes, but she also said that   it was the last time she was going to move, and she - 
she's sort of held her - kept to her word ever since.   Now once we moved to Cambridge it was not all 
smooth sailing: we had made an initial breakthrough   around the time we moved, but we ran into all sorts 
of technical difficulties and before we arrived at   something that was interpretable, and this is how 
Brian Wimberly spent his year and a half   in Cambridge in front of a graphics screen, with 
stereo glasses, building the ribosome structure.   Now in order to get the data that we needed we had 
to use these very powerful X-ray sources called   synchrotrons and you can see they're all over 
the place from near New York to in the mountains   of near the Alps and France to Chicago in the 
north of England, so I'd like to say you know,   you can join a ribosome lab and see the world, 
but just like the Navy, you know, said 'join the   U.S. Navy and see the world'  I was lying, too, because 
like the Navy, you're not seeing the world, you're   seeing some inside of some horrible battleship and 
and they're not saying you know - the - you know, the -   they're not going skiing or going to jazz concerts 
in Chicago or New York they're actually seeing the   inside of the synchrotrons and collect and staying 
up day and night to collect data. Now, for one of   them for the actual crucial experiment, we needed 
to align our crystals so that pair spots which was   symmetric would show up on the same image. There's 
a technical reason for that that I described in   the book and there's only one beam line at the 
time that could do that sort of alignment and   also have - was bright enough in terms of X-rays 
to produce good data, and that was at the ApS   in Chicago and I want to - I want to point this out - 
I've described this in the book because it shows   you how chancy and how quirky science can be - so in 
October of 1999 I decided we were - we had reached   the limits of what we could do elsewhere and I wrote to 
the people in at the beamline in Chicago at the   APS and said 'can you give us beam time?' and we had 
already published an intermediate version of our   structure, so we had some credibility by that time. 
And I got some - I got a reply saying that you know,   there's no time now but there would be time early 
the next year, and he would get back to me. Well, a   month later I repeated my request and didn't get 
a reply; then in a month later I phoned the person   and left a message on their voicemail, and there 
was still no reply. Then by this time the Yale   group had cracked their structure and in early 
January of 2000, Peter Moore wrote to me saying   he was visiting England and could he give a talk? 
So I used the opportunity to ask Peter whether   he would talk to his colleague Paul Ziegler who 
was a famous structural biologist who was on the   committee that oversaw this instrument in - in 
the synchrotron and whether he could put in a   word for us? So a day later Peter wrote back saying 
that he's going to talk to Paul - which sounds sort   of Biblical and actually considering the effect 
it had on me, it really was - and - and then a day   later, Peter wrote saying that Paul had contacted 
the people and - and the beamline and they would get   back to me and then a day later the person at the 
beamline wrote back saying he was sorry, it had   been really busy but you know, he was willing to 
give me time at the end of March so this was six   months after I had originally asked for beam time 
in a what actually was a very tight race. Okay - I   mean all the papers came out within weeks of each 
other and you know the really sad thing is four -   four days after this intervention, Paul Sigler 
died of a heart attack on his way to work - okay?   and this shows two things, you know: in science, it 
helps if you know people who know people who know   people, okay? - if you're part of that inner circle 
you know things can happen that - you know, if I   had been out of that inner circle there'd be nobody 
who could intercede on my behalf. The second thing   is this weird quirk which is if I'd waited two 
weeks there would have been no Paul Sigler to   intercede on my behalf - and I'm not sure what would 
have happened then - so you know this just goes to   show you how chancy science can be, how it can 
really depend on, you know, the vagaries of fate - so   anyway we went off to Chicago and over a 48- hour 
non-stop data collection period, we collected our   data and I talked about what that was like, and 
then what you end up is with a - you end up with   a three-dimensional image of the molecule if 
everything works, - and - but this doesn't tell you   what the structure is in chemical terms, it's just 
telling you what the three-dimensional image is. You   have to somehow interpret this three-dimensional 
image in chemical terms to build a molecule and   it's a little bit like solving a large, complicated 
three-dimensional jigsaw puzzle but without an   answer in the cover of the box, okay? And the way you 
would do that is you would do what you do for any   other jigsaw puzzle which is you'd look for areas 
that you recognize and what you see is you can   if you look over there you can see that there are 
these - these ridges that seem to be connected with   each other, and if you know what you're doing 
then these ridges are essentially two strands   of RNA that are interwoven around each other to 
form a double helix - RNA also forms double helix -  is like DNA  - so suddenly you've taken a piece of 
the image and you've built an atomic structure.   you've determined an atomic structure so now 
you know what a small piece of your molecule   looks like in atomic terms, and chemical terms, and 
now you have to do that for the whole image, and   so that's what you end up doing. And this involved 
spending months and months, in those days, in front   of the graphics terminal. And you can see that 
you end up building the entire structure and as   we solve the structure of the small subunit, the 
Yale group saw the structure of the large subunit,   and what these structures show is that the parts 
of the ribosome that make the stitch together   the protein from the amino acids, or the part 
that actually reads the code, consists entirely   of RNA, okay? and this solved -helped solve a 
long-standing puzzle which is if the ribosome   is made of both RNA and proteins but it's used 
to make proteins, where could the ribosome even   have come from? and Francis Crick was the first 
to suggest that maybe early ribosomes consisted   entirely of RNA and then people like Harry Noller
did experiments to suggest that that might be the   case - but it wasn't until the structures came out 
that we really knew that really the ribosome is   a - an RNA- based machine. Then a few years later we 
solved the high-resolution structure of the entire   ribosome with the genetic message and with the 
tRNA is bound and this led to a number of things,   including lots of snapshots of the ribosome 
in action to see how it worked, but also how   antibiotics bind to the ribosome and  -you know - 
we've solved structures of several antibiotics   bound to the ribosome and this is now leading to 
people trying to design better antibiotics which   have better properties and perhaps less toxicity 
and more effective against resistant bacteria.   Now some of the book also talks about the human 
side of science and scientists are neither Spocks   nor saints, you know: we're driven by curiosity, 
by ambition, by a desire to succeed, to be first.   We have egos, we have insecurities, with anxiety 
with this both rivalry and competition. We're   often motivated by self-interest - will collaborate 
or compete depending on what's - you know - best for   us. Sometimes competition is best; sometimes 
collaboration is best. But it's not all that   sort of thing because just as humans are complex, 
with a mixture of altruism and competition and   ego, that's the same as true in science. Many people 
help me: they gave me advice, they provided reagents,   technical help, and all of this was without any 
expectation - none of them wanted any money, they   didn't want to be co-authors on my paper. It was 
simply to advance the cause of science so you   saw the both, the best and the worst of humanity, 
in this microcosm of science. Now, thrown into the   microcosm is the whole politics of recognition 
and the corruption starts early with, you know,   prizes. I talked about how prizes apply what I call 
a sports metaphor to science. In sports, you know if   you have a hundred meter dash the rules are very 
well-defined, the goal is very well-defined and   the measurement is very well-defined, so you know 
who came first, second and third; but that's not   true in a scientific problem. Scientific problems 
are multi-dimensional - different people contribute   to different aspects of the problem. Even for the 
same sort of thing, it's hard to - often hard to know   who made the really key - who had the key idea, the 
key experiment, that advanced it, you know - so - and   unfortunately we start this corruption very early. 
We start giving people prizes even in school, you   know, from undergraduate, graduate school and then 
we give young scientists early career   awards - we pluck out people we think are stars and 
give them early career awards, then you know they   start, you know, wanting to get elected to academies, 
and then they hanker after major awards and then   it's complicated by the fact that many awards 
imitate the Nobel Prize in a slavish way   by limiting it to three people. You know the Lasker 
Award, which is America's Nobel, used to have no   such limit, and now it does, in a slavish imitation 
of the Nobel Prize you know  -and - and that's very -   you know in 1901 maybe it was reasonable; it's 
certainly not reasonable today when lots and   lots of people contribute to different prob -
to a particular problem. Another thing is the   public regards the Nobel Prize as a sign of genius, 
okay? but reality - the reality is more like how   Shakespeare described it:  some are born great, 
some achieve greatness, and some have greatness   thrust upon them. The same is true of Nobel Prizes. 
You know -people - you know - stumble onto an important   discovery or maybe they're in the right place 
at the right time, or you know, they have great   colleagues who give them advice and they 
have resources, so it's all sorts of reasons,   but it's given not for being a great scientist - 
it's given for being - making an important discovery   or an advance - but because it's somehow - is because 
of its history it's been associated with greatness,   people hanker after the Nobel Prize and in my book 
I somewhat scathingly refer to two diseases called   "pre- Nobel - itis" which is when you've sort of feel 
you're in contention for the Nobel Prize and you   get this anxiety every October and so on and -and 
then there's also "post- Nobel- itis"  - people who get   the Prize for work they did 20 years earlier and 
are long past their peak, are suddenly thrust into   the limelight and treated as geniuses and they go 
swanning around the world, you know, pontificating   about all sorts of things, including the future 
of humanity and so on - so - so but whatever it is,   people sort of hanker after these things and - and 
that warps their behavior and I talk about "the   ribosome road show" - there were several people 
you know depending on - you know - their own view of   themselves, partly - you know - some of them I think 
were delusional, but anyway - there were say half a   dozen people and  - so there are conferences 
all over the world and you know here we are we've   solved these structures and trotting around from 
one place to another talking of our work, and   so there's sort of  -this jockeying for three chairs 
you know, when the - when the music stops and it also   almost takes on the kind of characteristics of a 
political campaign and you know, suddenly I found   the Swedes were terribly interested in ribosomes, 
okay? - and they were - keep inviting us to   meetings in Sweden. It's clear we were being 
auditioned and at the last of these meetings -   which was on the entire what biologists called a 
'central dogma' which is how DNA replicates itself   and how it copies itself down copies into RNA 
and then how RNA is used to make proteins - so that   entire field people were invited to this meeting 
in Sweden. Now there was a guy at that meeting who   was the only Swedish ribosomologist to speak 
at the meeting and he was sort of miffed that we   had ignored his earlier work on in the 1970s on 
one aspect of the ribosome, so at the banquet on   the final night of the meeting he accosted me and 
started chewing me out for ignoring his work and   that and at some point I got annoyed and started 
you know arguing with him because actually I felt   his early work was mistaken, okay? and - and - and 
the argument got so heated that a colleague of his   had to come and separate us and so I went home, and 
then two months later I learned that he had been   appointed to the Nobel Committee on chemistry - 
so I decided that I was not going to accept any   more meetings to Sweden because why should I go 
there to be a supplicant and eat bad vegetarian   food when I don't have a chance, okay? So I didn't 
go there and then suddenly, five years later, in one   October when I showed up in the lab with a flat 
tire and somewhat irritable I - on my bicycle, that   is - and- and- and - and I showed up somewhat irritable 
and I got this phone call which said 'This   is an important call from Swedish Academy of 
Sciences' and I didn't believe it; I told the guy   'Look, you have a good Swedish accent but I'm not 
going to believe you until you put this guy Monson   Berg on the line" and - and sure enough Monce came on 
the line and said "thank you. I want to congratulate   you and I want to say you deserve it, but it's the 
last time I'm going to tell you that" so that was   my thing and so you know given my track record 
you know I didn't go to Harvard or Berkeley or   Caltech or any of these places: I went to my local 
hometown university in India; I've been to a state   university in southeastern Ohio and wandered 
around from physics to biology using strange   techniques -- I never imagined I would be 
in this situation one December - and I just want   to point out what is going on at this big table: 
this is the - just if you follow - look at just above   my dog - that's the king of Sweden, who's at the 
center of the table and this is Ada Yonath,  and that's me; that is the Crown Princess Victoria 
and this is Tom Steitz on - on the other side of her   and my wife Vera is busy in conversation with 
the German Vice-Chancellor. Now you know there're   about three science prizes or about nine Nobel 
laureates that year, and nine science laureates in   addition to two economists and a litera-  and a
Literature Prize winner, but clearly the Swedish   newspapers realized that the important thing 
that year was actually the ribosome  - much   more important than all those other prizes that 
they gave out that year - and why do I know that?   Because the next morning the thing that was on 
the front page of the Swedish newspapers was   this, okay? Tom Steitz and me had made the front 
page of the - of the Swedish newspapers - so clearly   they thought that the ribosome was the important 
thing. It had nothing to do with Crown Princess   Victoria sitting between us. Okay -  So in my last 
slide I wanted - looking back at my life, you know -  what did I do that somehow made it all work out? I 
could have fallen off the edge multiple times in   my life, okay? and I always had a plan B and a Plan 
C and so on - and one thing I did do was to keep my   options open so when physics didn't work out, I 
didn't sort of quit science, or take some sort of   you know - job that I didn't really want -- what I did 
was to start over again in biology. Then when I had   sort of a technique that really wasn't being 
very useful, I went on sabbatical to learn new   technique and then I was willing to move places to 
always keep myself - you know - give myself the best   environment in which to do my research. The other 
thing that I would say I have is that I'm never   afraid to ask for help - you know - I'm not worried 
about seeming ignorant, things like that, because   you know if you don't know something the best - 
the fastest - way is to really ask for help from   people who do know. And - and that involved talking 
to people but you can't also can't also talk to people   all the time, because then you'll be influenced 
by -you know- what's fashionable and so on, and you   won't really be able to do original work. The 
other thing you have to realize is everybody   has "impostor syndrome" - you know - I take a guy like 
me who went to sort of second-tier institutions   and then suddenly find myself postdoc at Yale, or 
going to the MRC Lab, or like [the?] biology - suddenly   you find you know 'my God I fooled these people and 
in two weeks they're gonna find out I'm a complete   idiot'  and you, everybody, has this unless 
they're sociopaths, okay? - they probably don't know -   and- and you know, most people have learned how to 
cover up and - and get along and  sometimes if you're an outsider you might feel 
it even more, and you know, if you're a minority   say you're - you're a woman and it's a field 
dominated by men you might feel, oh you know "maybe   I don't really belong here" or you're an outsider

depends on money to be able to do the sort of   work you want to do - it depends on skill, which 
depends on training, depends on patience, and it   depends also on luck - and I'd like to stop here 
but while we're taking questions I want to just   show you a movie of the ribosome in action -

support young scientists? ---Yeah - so what I'm working -   so you know in science when you solve a problem, it 
simply raises the next level of questions. So now   that we know what ribosomes look like, we're trying 
to understand how they work. Now we've done a lot   of work on how ribosomes actually do their thing, 
but now we want to understand how does the cell   regulate ribosomes? How does the cell decide when 
ribosomes are - are stuck and need to be rescued?   How do viruses hijack ribosomes to do their own - 
make their own proteins? How do ribosomes and  mitochondria - which are these organelles inside 
us - how do they function? So there are all sorts   of different questions that we're trying to 
answer and there are more powerful techniques   now which are like - which involve cryo-electron 
microscopy. So today nobody would go through all   this odyssey that - you know - I described; you would, 
you know, simply use electron microscopy to solve   structures. So that's the first part. The other part 
is how do you support students? Well, I never tell   students in my lab what they should work on; rather, 
they come to my lab and I say, 'Look, these are the   open questions in the field of translation (-which 
is ribosome protein, how proteins are made -) and you   know, why don't you think about it and - and pick 
something that you think is workable and then -  or come up with a couple of ideas, and here are 
some of - some things you might think about' - and   then they choose the thing, and when they choose 
it they feel it's their problem, and they feel   invested in it, and because - you know, science is 
hard. A lot of times things - most of the time, things   are not working and they have to feel motivated 
to - to come in every day and do their thing.  --I was   here [?] Thank you for the talk. No question - 
you mentioned about sometimes it's very difficult   to identify who made an - a key concept or key 
contributions. How - how do you fix that? For example,   go back to '50s when James Watson and Francis Crick 
discovered DNA - a graduate student and a postdoc   come publish here, a paper in Nature. And now it is 
impossible for graduate students and postdocs. -Yes -- How   do you fix this problem? -- I would say you know one thing 
is that of all the prizes oddly enough the Nobel   Prize is the - is one of the few big prizes that 
recognizes actual contributions by students. For   example, the telomerase prize went to Carol Greider; 
you know - she shared the prize for work that she   did as a graduate student with Liz Blackburn; or 
George Kohler was a postdoc with César Milstein   and shared the Nobel Prize for antibodies;  but in 
general it is a big problem, okay? and part of it   is that I think the view is that - you know - the 
students and postdocs are there to be trained   but really it's the lab head who's setting the 
agenda, who has the vision, and is really driving   the problem forward, and so that is a particular view,
it's not always true, okay? and it's certainly true   that the - the- the lab head would not be able to do 
it with students - without students or postdocs - it's   very, very rare - maybe in theoretical physics or 
something but in experimental biology that's very,   very rare. However, there might be a view that well -
if it hadn't been for this student or that student,   some other student might have come in and done 
the same sort of work - so that's the - the kind of   view that the prize juries take but I - as I point 
out in the book, it is - I do actually point out   this particular problem, which is that it doesn't 
recognize, you know, that science is done    by teams and not by - necessarily by individuals. --We 
just have time for a couple more questions.  --Oh hi -   so I'm a freshman, first-year undergrad right now - 
yeah - I just want to ask if you have one piece of   advice for on - you know, early-stage students like 
undergrad students who are looking to maybe going to   like biochemistry or cell biology in the future - 
in the next, you know, 10, 20 years? --So - sorry - what   did you want? -- So if you have any advice for like - so 
I'm a first-year undergrad right now so you have   any advice for like- uh- younger students who want to 
go into like biochemistry or cell biology in the   next...?  --So the one thing I would say is try to see 
what it is that fascinates you and interests you   and don't worry too much about fads, okay? If you're 
a first- year undergrad - you say you're an undergrad,   okay, you're first - year undergrad, you have three 
more years of undergrad, then you've got maybe   four or five years of graduate school, then three 
or four years of postdoc - we're talking 10 - 15 years   down the line before you start work. So whatever 
you think is really - whatever you think is really   fashionable now may not be fashionable in 10 or 
15 years, for various reasons: it might be people   might realize it's not that important, or it might 
actually be finished, you know, and the interesting   problems might be solved. So I would say just stay 
interested and stay open at each stage of your   life, okay? As an undergrad you should be sampling 
very widely, and I would say you should acquire a   very good background in what I call the three 
or four core disciplines: mathematics, which under -   underpins all of science, you know; physics, 
which also underpins, you know, a lot of science -   and chemistry, and - and then biology, so I would 
say if you train well in those three or four   disciplines that gives you the tools later to 
enter almost any area of science. --Hi, I am over here -   um - I have a question. So you said that you were in 
a competition with the Yale team - so did you guys   ever collaborate with each other or like bounce 
ideas off of each other? --No, you know- so we - we   had a question before about -you know, competition, and 
especially with Ada - and I think you know part of   it was that - you know Ada had the field to herself 
for a long time and so she was not entirely happy   with all these other people coming in to what 
she sort of felt was her domain, okay? So relations   were not great and with the Yale group and us, you 
know, we - we were friendly because one of them was   my former mentor, Peter Moore, so we remained on 
friendly terms - where we weren't quite so direct   competitors because they were working on the other 
subunit - but nevertheless, you know, because it was   important that we get there first we 
weren't all that communicative either, okay? We   were not exactly, you know, open about it - and that - 
that is a problem with this sort of -  you know, not   all of science is a race. Most of science isn't 
like a race where the goal is very well-defined and   it's a question of who gets there first: a lot of 
science is very open-ended and - and exploratory, so   you mustn't get the idea that it's always like 
this but often it's like that, you know; I mean   some - once a problem becomes known to be important 
then it becomes a goal, and then it becomes like   that. --Last question in this top corner - right up 
here:  Hi  --Hi - was - was the concern about [?] and new   antibiotics something that you were working with 
even as a younger scientist - what its relationship   to the ribosome - or is that a new concern?  --The 
relationship of  -- Well, the antibiotics were very useful   because you could write on NIH grants that lots 
of antibiotics bind to the ribosome and therefore   if you understand the ribosome you might one day 
help get better antibiotics - and for 20 years we   wrote this without believing it - but it turned out 
to be true, okay? by-by - completely by accident we   realized we were actually telling the truth - and - 
and so now, we do know how antibiotics bind but   that's not what motivated us initially, okay? What 
motivated us was a central problem in biology. [question muted]  --I think the advances in computing have always 
gone hand in hand with advances in structural   biology so from the very first protein structures 
they used the state-of-the-art computing of their   time, you know, which was an EDSAC or something 
like that which took up an entire room and as   computing - as the problems in biology kept 
getting more complex the computing became   more powerful, so we're always sort of ending 
up using the most powerful computers that there   are. ----And I think that's all the time we have 
for this evening -- one last round of applause.

Ramakrishnan is a senior scientist at the MRC laboratory of molecular biology in Cambridge England he received the 2009 Nobel Prize in Chemistry together with doctors a do Nath and Tom Stites for their work on deciphering the structure and function of the ribosome so in a sense he is the first of many translational researchers who will speak from this podium as the scientist Venki was a late bloomer in his own words he discovered girls before he discovered theoretical physics born in India he got his early education in physics as an undergraduate there and then came to the US where he obtained his PhD in physics from Ohio University following his PhD he transitioned to biology during a postdoc at UCSD in the early 1980s Venki as a postdoc in Peter Moore's lab at Yale initiated studies of the ribosome by Neutron diffraction after finding a research staff position at Brookhaven National Laboratory another of our sister federal labs minke taught himself x-ray crystallography and determined the structures of several protein subunits of the ribosome it was not until 1995 when Venki moved to the University of Utah that he launched his structural studies of the 30s subunit of the ribosome the rest as we say is history in addition to the Nobel Prize Venky's elegant work has been recognized by election to the National Academy of Sciences and the Royal Society and by several other prestigious awards today thanking will tell us about his beautiful structural and functional studies of the ribosome his talk is entitled what we have learned from structures of the ribosome dr. Ramakrishnan thank you very much it's a real pleasure to be here the first time I spoke first and only other time I've spoken here was in 2000 when there was a trio of asada Ionut and Peter Moore and I gave the Stetten lecture and I have to say we've learned a lot more about ribosome function since then although that was really a watershed year so I'll I want to say that I've been supportive for many decades by the NIH so when I was invited to give this talk I really didn't feel I could refuse because the NIH has supported me from the time I was a graduate student trying to make that transition into biology all the way until 2006 actually I also am grateful to these people because these are the people first joining me in Utah the first four and then continuing on at the MRC who actually risk their careers in working on these very challenging projects and it's okay for a tenured group leader or faculty member to try and tackle hard things because you know if it fails you still have a job but for people who are students or postdocs to try and do these things takes real courage and so I always like to start by thanking the people who actually not only did the work but also made a lot of the intellectual contributions to what I'm going to tell you since this is a general talk I thought I would tell you a little bit about how people approached ribosome structure and how we sort of got to where we are today and then talk about our own work on decoding and finishing up with some very recent work that we published only late last year so if you look at what the ribosome has to do it as Michael pointed out to do with translation which is very useful because the MRC is like the NIH is very interested in research translational research so I like to say I've
